title: Policies for information security
description: Management-approved information security policies must be defined, published, and communicated to all relevant staff and interested parties.
guidance: Draft a top-level information security policy signed by the CISO or equivalent, reviewed annually and communicated via intranet or onboarding.
implementationGuide: Start by drafting a single top-level Information Security Policy signed by your CEO or CISO, clearly stating the organisation's commitment to protecting information assets and delegating authority downward. Publish the policy in Confluence or SharePoint and configure a mandatory annual acknowledgement workflow — tools like Vanta or Drata can automate this, sending reminders and recording completions in an audit-ready log. Beneath the top-level policy, maintain topic-specific sub-policies (acceptable use, access control, incident response, data classification) each owned by a named role and stored in the same document repository with version history enabled. Set a recurring calendar event tied to the policy owner's account to trigger the annual review cycle no later than 30 days before the anniversary of the last approval. The observable done state is a signed, dated policy document published to all staff, with evidence of communication (email broadcast or intranet post) and a completion log showing >95% staff acknowledgement. The most common audit failure mode is a policy that was approved once but has not been formally reviewed in over 12 months — auditors will check the 'last reviewed' date and request the review minutes or change log.
exampleEvidence: Signed Information Security Policy document bearing CEO/CISO signature dated within the last 12 months, with version number and review date
Confluence or SharePoint page revision history showing annual review edits with named approver and date
Vanta or Drata compliance dashboard screenshot confirming 100% staff policy acknowledgement for current policy version
Email broadcast or intranet announcement confirming policy was communicated to all staff with send date and recipient count
Policy register spreadsheet listing all sub-policies, their owners, last review dates, and next scheduled review dates
Board or management meeting minutes containing agenda item approving the information security policy for the current year

title: Information security roles and responsibilities
description: Roles and responsibilities for information security must be defined and allocated according to the organisation's needs.
guidance: Create an RACI matrix mapping each security domain to named roles and include it in job descriptions.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a formal RACI matrix that maps every ISO 27001 control domain to a named role — not a person, but a role title — so accountability survives staff turnover. Publish this matrix in Confluence and link it from your ISMS scope document so auditors can trace it directly. Use your HRIS (e.g., BambooHR or Workday) to embed security responsibilities into job descriptions for all roles with elevated access or data handling duties, ensuring these are signed by employees at onboarding. Assign an Information Security Manager or CISO role with a documented charter that includes authority to enforce controls, escalate to the board, and request resources. Review the RACI annually or after any significant structural change such as a merger, acquisition, or major headcount shift. The done state is a published RACI matrix with named role owners, linked job descriptions containing security clauses, and board-level visibility of the security function. The most common audit failure mode is a RACI that names individuals who have since left the organisation, leaving controls with no active owner.
exampleEvidence: RACI matrix document in Confluence mapping all 93 ISO 27001 controls to named role owners, dated and version-controlled
Signed job description for the Information Security Manager/CISO role including explicit security accountability clauses
Board resolution or management charter document formally establishing the security function with named authority
HRIS (BambooHR or Workday) screenshots showing security responsibilities embedded in at least three role-specific job descriptions
Org chart showing the security function reporting line to senior management, exported from the current HRIS
Annual RACI review sign-off email or meeting minutes confirming all role owners are current employees in the stated roles

title: Segregation of duties
description: Conflicting duties and areas of responsibility must be segregated to reduce opportunities for unauthorised or unintentional modification of assets.
guidance: Identify high-risk role combinations (e.g. developer + production access) and enforce separation via access controls and approvals.
implementationGuide: Start by identifying all processes where a single individual could initiate and approve a transaction, provision and audit their own access, or both develop and deploy code — these are your segregation of duties (SoD) conflict hotspots. Map these conflicts in a SoD matrix and then enforce controls in your IAM platform: in Okta or Microsoft Entra ID, configure role assignments so that no single account can hold conflicting entitlements simultaneously, and generate alerts when exceptions are granted. For financial systems like NetSuite or Xero, use built-in SoD reports or a GRC overlay like Vanta to flag violations. Where genuine SoD is impossible due to team size, implement compensating controls such as enhanced logging in your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) and mandatory management review of all actions taken by dual-role individuals. Document approved exceptions formally — each one needs a named approver, a rationale, a compensating control, and a review date. The done state is a published SoD matrix with zero unmitigated conflicts, enforced technically where possible and with documented exceptions where not. Auditors most commonly find failures when exceptions are granted verbally without documentation, or when access reviews do not flag inherited conflicts from role changes.
exampleEvidence: SoD conflict matrix spreadsheet identifying all high-risk role combinations across IT, finance, and HR systems
Okta or Entra ID access policy configuration screenshot showing mutually exclusive role groups preventing SoD conflicts
SoD exception register with named approver, compensating control description, and review date for each approved exception
SIEM (Splunk or Sentinel) alert rule configuration showing monitoring of dual-role accounts for high-risk actions
Quarterly access review report from Vanta or manual review confirming no unmitigated SoD conflicts exist
Financial system (NetSuite or Xero) built-in SoD report exported showing zero unmitigated conflicts in the current quarter

title: Management responsibilities
description: Management must require all personnel to apply information security in accordance with the organisation's established policies and procedures.
guidance: Include information security compliance as a standing item in performance reviews and team meetings.
implementationGuide: Start by ensuring your executive leadership team has formally acknowledged the information security policy and their individual responsibilities within it — this is typically captured in board minutes or a signed management statement. Use your ISMS governance framework to schedule quarterly security steering committee meetings attended by at least one C-level sponsor, with documented agendas and minutes stored in Confluence. Embed security KPIs (e.g., patch compliance rate, phishing simulation click rate, open critical vulnerabilities) into management dashboards using tools like Vanta or a custom Power BI report fed from your vulnerability scanner and SIEM. Require managers to actively enforce compliance within their teams: this means following up on overdue security training, escalating unresolved risks, and participating in incident response tabletop exercises. The done state is a set of board/steering committee minutes from the last four quarters each containing a security agenda item, plus management-signed acknowledgement of security responsibilities. The most common audit failure is a security programme that exists on paper but where no management-level meeting minutes reference security — auditors look for evidence that leadership is actively engaged, not just nominally responsible.
exampleEvidence: Board or steering committee meeting minutes from the last four quarters each containing an information security agenda item
Signed management statement confirming executive acknowledgement of information security responsibilities and policy
Security KPI dashboard (Power BI or Vanta) screenshot showing metrics reported to management in the current quarter
Email chain or calendar invite showing C-level executive as a standing attendee at the quarterly security review meeting
Risk register extract showing management sign-off on accepted risks with named executive owner for each item
Tabletop exercise attendance record signed by at least one member of senior management within the last 12 months

title: Contact with authorities
description: The organisation must establish and maintain contact with relevant authorities (e.g. regulators, law enforcement) regarding information security matters.
guidance: Document a contacts register for ICO, NCSC, and sector regulators, and assign ownership of each relationship.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a contact register that maps each relevant authority type (national cybersecurity agency, data protection regulator, law enforcement, sector-specific regulator) to a named internal owner responsible for maintaining that relationship. For UK organisations, this typically means registering with the ICO and noting your NCSC contact points; for EU entities, identify your lead supervisory authority under GDPR. Store contact details and reporting procedures in your incident response runbook in Confluence so that when an incident occurs, the right person knows exactly who to call and within what timeframe. Configure your ticketing system (Jira or ServiceNow) to include a mandatory 'regulatory notification required?' field in all P1/P2 incident tickets, with a 72-hour breach notification SLA timer if GDPR applies. Review the contact register at least annually and after any regulatory change — subscribe to NCSC and ICO newsletters to catch updates. The done state is a maintained contact register reviewed in the last 12 months, embedded in the incident response runbook, with evidence of at least one test of the notification process. Auditors commonly find failures when the contact register exists but is not linked to the incident response process, meaning contacts are never actually used.
exampleEvidence: Regulatory contact register listing authority name, contact details, reporting obligations, and named internal owner, reviewed within 12 months
Incident response runbook in Confluence showing regulatory notification decision tree with 72-hour GDPR breach notification SLA
ICO registration certificate or equivalent data protection authority registration confirmation
Jira or ServiceNow incident ticket template screenshot showing mandatory 'regulatory notification required' field
Evidence of subscription to NCSC, ICO, or sector regulator mailing lists (screenshot of subscription confirmation emails)
Annual contact register review sign-off document with reviewer name, date, and confirmation that all contacts were verified as current

title: Contact with special interest groups
description: The organisation must maintain contact with special interest groups, security forums, and professional associations for awareness of the threat landscape.
guidance: Subscribe to NCSC, CISA, and at least one sector-specific ISAC mailing list; document membership.
implementationGuide: Start by identifying the two or three special interest groups most relevant to your industry — for most organisations this means ISAC membership (e.g., FS-ISAC for financial services, H-ISAC for healthcare), CREST or CIISec membership, and participation in NCSC's Cyber Security Information Sharing Partnership (CiSP). Assign a named owner for each membership who is responsible for consuming threat intelligence feeds, attending forums, and sharing anonymised threat data back to the community. Document the value derived from each membership in your quarterly security steering committee report — this demonstrates active engagement rather than passive membership. Use threat intelligence from these groups to feed your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) by importing Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) from ISAC feeds into your threat intelligence platform (e.g., MISP or Recorded Future). The done state is active membership in at least two groups with documented evidence of intelligence consumed and acted upon in the last 12 months. The most common audit failure is paying for membership but having no evidence that intelligence was reviewed or used to improve controls.
exampleEvidence: ISAC or sector group membership certificate or confirmation letter dated within the last 12 months
CiSP (NCSC) membership profile screenshot showing active status and at least one contribution or consumption event
SIEM (Splunk or Sentinel) IoC import log showing threat intelligence from an ISAC feed applied within the last quarter
Quarterly security steering committee report extract referencing intelligence received from a special interest group
Email or forum post showing participation in a special interest group threat briefing or working group within 12 months
MISP or Recorded Future threat intelligence platform dashboard showing active feeds from external communities

title: Threat intelligence
description: Information relating to information security threats must be collected and analysed to produce threat intelligence.
guidance: Integrate a threat intelligence feed (commercial or free) into the SIEM and establish a weekly review process.
implementationGuide: Start by subscribing your security team to at least two authoritative threat intelligence feeds relevant to your sector — NCSC alerts, CISA advisories, and a commercial feed such as Recorded Future or Mandiant Advantage are a good baseline. Configure your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) to ingest IoCs from these feeds automatically and generate alerts when internal traffic matches known malicious indicators. Establish a weekly threat review cadence where the security team assesses new intelligence, determines relevance to your threat model, and decides whether a control change is warranted — document decisions and rationale in Confluence. Feed threat intelligence outputs into your vulnerability management process: when a new TTP (Tactic, Technique, Procedure) emerges, cross-reference it against your vulnerability scanner (Tenable, Qualys) to identify exposed assets. The done state is an active threat intelligence programme with documented feeds, a regular review cadence with written outputs, and evidence that intelligence has influenced at least one control or remediation decision in the last quarter. Auditors most commonly fail organisations that subscribe to feeds but have no documented process for reviewing or acting on intelligence.
exampleEvidence: Recorded Future or MISP threat intelligence platform subscription confirmation with active feed configuration screenshot
Splunk or Sentinel IoC ingestion log showing automatic import of threat feeds within the last 30 days
Weekly threat review meeting minutes or Confluence page documenting assessed threats, relevance ratings, and action decisions
Vulnerability scanner (Tenable or Qualys) remediation ticket generated in response to a threat intelligence finding
NCSC or CISA advisory email showing subscription confirmation and at least one advisory actioned within the last quarter
Threat intelligence programme procedure document defining feeds, review cadence, escalation path, and integration with vulnerability management

title: Information security in project management
description: Information security must be integrated into project management throughout the project lifecycle.
guidance: Add a mandatory security impact assessment checkpoint to the project governance process.
implementationGuide: Start by embedding information security as a mandatory workstream in your project methodology — whether that is PRINCE2, Agile/Scrum, or an internal framework — so that no project can move to production without a completed security review gate. Configure your project tracking tool (Jira or Microsoft Project) to include a security sign-off field that is required before the 'Ready for Production' status can be selected. Develop a lightweight security checklist for project managers covering data classification, third-party integrations, access control design, and data retention — store it in Confluence and link it from the project template. Require a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for any project involving personal data, integrated into the project kickoff process and reviewed by your DPO. Engage the security team at project initiation (not at go-live) so that risks can be designed out rather than bolted on. The done state is a project register showing 100% of projects in the last 12 months have a completed security review gate with named approver. Auditors most commonly find failures where security reviews are skipped for 'urgent' or 'small' projects that later turn out to have significant data exposure.
exampleEvidence: Jira or project tracking tool screenshot showing mandatory security sign-off field completed on a recently delivered project
Security checklist template in Confluence with completed example from a live project showing all fields answered
DPIA completed for a project involving personal data, signed by the DPO and stored with the project documentation
Project register showing all projects delivered in the last 12 months with security review status column completed
Project methodology document (PRINCE2 or Agile framework) extract showing security review as a mandatory gate
Security team sign-off email or ticket closure confirming approval for production deployment of a specific named project

title: Inventory of information and other associated assets
description: An inventory of information and associated assets, including owners, must be identified and maintained.
guidance: Maintain a living asset register in a CMDB or spreadsheet, reviewed quarterly, with a named owner per asset.
implementationGuide: Start by building your asset inventory in a dedicated CMDB or asset management tool — ServiceNow, Lansweeper, or the asset module within your GRC platform (Vanta, Drata) — and populate it by running an automated network discovery scan using Nmap or your vulnerability scanner (Tenable, Qualys) to identify all devices. For information assets (data stores, file shares, SaaS applications), conduct a data flow mapping exercise with each business unit to identify what data is held where, then record each asset with its classification, owner, and location. Assign an asset owner to every entry — this is a named role, not just a team — and configure your tool to alert you when assets have not been reviewed in the last 12 months. Integrate your asset inventory with your vulnerability management programme so that new assets discovered by the scanner are automatically added to the CMDB for ownership assignment. The done state is a complete asset register reviewed within the last 12 months with every asset having a named owner and classification. Auditors most commonly find failures when shadow IT assets (SaaS tools procured without IT involvement) are absent from the register.
exampleEvidence: Lansweeper or ServiceNow CMDB export showing all assets with owner, classification, location, and last review date
Tenable or Qualys asset discovery scan report showing all discovered network assets cross-referenced against the CMDB
Data flow diagram or data mapping document showing information assets, their classification, storage location, and business owner
Vanta or Drata asset inventory dashboard screenshot showing total asset count and percentage with assigned owners
Asset ownership assignment email chain or HRIS workflow showing named owners accepting responsibility for their assets
SaaS application inventory report from a tool like Torii or BetterCloud showing all cloud applications in use with owner and data classification

title: Acceptable use of information and other associated assets
description: Rules for the acceptable use and handling of information and associated assets must be identified, documented, and implemented.
guidance: Publish an acceptable use policy covering devices, email, internet, and cloud services; require annual sign-off.
implementationGuide: Start by publishing an Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) in Confluence or SharePoint that explicitly covers use of corporate devices, personal devices for work (BYOD), corporate email, internet, cloud storage, and social media. Require all staff to sign or digitally acknowledge the AUP at onboarding and annually thereafter — use Vanta or your HR system (BambooHR) to track and report completion. Configure endpoint management tools (Microsoft Intune or Jamf) to enforce technical controls aligned to the policy: blocking USB storage, enforcing screen lock timeouts, and restricting installation of unapproved software via allow-listing. For cloud storage, configure Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace DLP policies to prevent sharing of classified data outside the organisation without approval. The done state is a current AUP acknowledged by >95% of staff, with technical controls enforced through endpoint management and DLP aligned to policy rules. Auditors most commonly find failures when the AUP exists but has no enforcement mechanism — policies without teeth fail the 'effectively implemented' test.
exampleEvidence: Signed or digitally acknowledged AUP report from Vanta or BambooHR showing 95%+ completion rate for all current employees
Microsoft Intune or Jamf configuration profile screenshot showing USB restriction, screen lock, and software restriction policies
Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace DLP policy configuration showing data classification-based sharing restrictions
AUP document in Confluence with version history, current approval date, and named policy owner
Endpoint compliance report from Intune or Jamf showing percentage of devices in compliance with AUP-derived technical controls
New employee onboarding checklist extract showing AUP sign-off as a mandatory pre-access step with completion tracked in HRIS

title: Return of assets
description: Personnel and other interested parties must return all organisational assets upon change or termination of employment or contract.
guidance: Create a leaver's checklist that includes return of all devices, tokens, and access cards before final payroll.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a formal asset return procedure that is triggered automatically whenever an employee offboarding is initiated in your HRIS (BambooHR, Workday, or HiBob). The procedure must list every category of asset that must be returned — laptops, mobile phones, access cards, hardware tokens, corporate credit cards — with sign-off required from IT, facilities, and the employee's manager before final payroll processing. Integrate this with your MDM solution (Microsoft Intune or Jamf) so that the device is enrolled and trackable throughout its lifecycle, and configure automated remote wipe if a device is not returned within 5 business days of the leaver's last day. Use a physical asset register with serial numbers to reconcile returned equipment against issued equipment at each offboarding event. The done state is a documented offboarding checklist with asset return sign-off for every leaver in the last 12 months, cross-referenced against the asset register. Auditors most commonly find failures where laptops are returned but soft assets (remote access tokens, VPN certificates) are not revoked as part of the same process.
exampleEvidence: Completed offboarding checklist for a recent leaver showing asset return sign-off from IT, facilities, and line manager
Microsoft Intune or Jamf device inventory showing returned device status updated to 'decommissioned' or 'returned' with date
Asset register extract showing serial number reconciliation between issued and returned assets for the last quarter's leavers
HRIS (BambooHR or Workday) offboarding workflow screenshot showing asset return as a mandatory step before final payroll processing
Remote wipe confirmation log from Intune or Jamf for a device that was not returned within the agreed timeframe
Signed asset return receipt from an employee's last day, co-signed by the IT asset manager with list of returned items and serial numbers

title: Classification of information
description: Information must be classified according to the information security needs of the organisation based on confidentiality, integrity, and availability requirements.
guidance: Define at least four classification levels (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) and publish a handling guide.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a classification scheme with no more than four levels — typically Public, Internal, Confidential, and Restricted — and document the criteria for each level in a data classification policy published in Confluence. Map existing data stores to classification levels by running a data discovery exercise using Microsoft Purview, Varonis, or your DLP tool to identify where sensitive data currently resides. Train all staff on classification at onboarding and annually, using your LMS (KnowBe4, Mimecast) with a short scenario-based quiz to confirm understanding. Configure Microsoft Purview or Google Workspace sensitivity labels to align with your classification scheme so that users are prompted to apply labels when creating documents. The done state is a published classification scheme with technical labelling enforced in your document management platform and >90% of staff trained. Auditors most commonly find failures when the classification scheme has too many levels (five or more), making it too complex for staff to use consistently, resulting in misclassified data.
exampleEvidence: Data classification policy document in Confluence defining four classification levels with criteria, handling rules, and named owner
Microsoft Purview or Google Workspace sensitivity label configuration screenshot showing labels aligned to the classification scheme
Data discovery scan report from Varonis or Purview identifying unclassified sensitive data stores requiring remediation
KnowBe4 or Mimecast LMS report showing >90% staff completion of data classification awareness module with quiz pass rates
Sample document with correct sensitivity label applied, demonstrating the labelling workflow in Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace
Classification scheme review sign-off from CISO or DPO confirming scheme remains fit for purpose, dated within 12 months

title: Labelling of information
description: An appropriate set of procedures for information labelling must be developed and implemented in accordance with the classification scheme.
guidance: Enforce classification labels in email headers and document headers via DLP tooling or policy templates.
implementationGuide: Start by defining labelling standards for each classification level — digital documents should carry a sensitivity label header/footer applied by Microsoft Purview or Google Workspace labels, and physical documents should be marked with a visible classification stamp at the top and bottom of each page. Configure auto-labelling policies in Microsoft Purview to detect sensitive content types (e.g., PII, financial data, credentials) and apply the appropriate label automatically, reducing reliance on user judgement. For email, configure DLP rules that block or warn users when sending a Confidential or Restricted document externally without encryption. Run a quarterly audit using Purview's content explorer to identify labelled vs. unlabelled documents across SharePoint and Teams. The done state is a technical labelling system active for all document creation workflows with an auto-labelling policy covering major sensitive data types, evidenced by a quarterly labelling compliance report. Auditors most commonly find failures when labelling is purely manual and the organisation cannot demonstrate consistent application — sample testing during audits quickly exposes uneven labelling.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Purview auto-labelling policy configuration screenshot showing sensitive content types mapped to classification labels
Purview content explorer report showing percentage of documents with applied sensitivity labels across SharePoint and Teams
DLP policy configuration in Microsoft 365 showing rule that blocks external sharing of Restricted-labelled documents without encryption
Sample labelled email with sensitivity label header visible in both the email client and the forwarded copy
Physical document labelling standard SOP showing required stamp positions and label wording for each classification level
Quarterly labelling compliance report showing trend of labelled document coverage increasing toward the 95% target

title: Information transfer
description: Information transfer rules, procedures, and controls must be in place for transfers within and outside the organisation.
guidance: Prohibit unencrypted email for Confidential+ data and provide a secure file-transfer mechanism for external sharing.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a set of information transfer agreements and procedures that cover each transfer channel your organisation uses: email, file sharing (SharePoint, SFTP, managed file transfer), physical courier, and verbal communications. For email, configure Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace to enforce TLS 1.2+ for all outbound email and add a mandatory encryption step for Confidential/Restricted content using Microsoft Purview encryption or PGP. For file sharing with third parties, standardise on a secure managed file transfer platform (GoAnywhere MFT, Kiteworks) rather than allowing ad-hoc consumer tools. Publish an information transfer policy in Confluence specifying approved channels by classification level, and train staff on which channels are prohibited for sensitive data (e.g., WhatsApp for Confidential data). The done state is a published transfer policy, technical enforcement of TLS on email, and a secure file transfer platform in use for all third-party exchanges involving Confidential or Restricted data. Auditors most commonly find failures where staff are using consumer cloud storage (personal Dropbox, personal Google Drive) to share sensitive data because the approved channel is inconvenient.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace mail flow rule configuration showing mandatory TLS enforcement for all outbound email
GoAnywhere MFT or Kiteworks platform user list and transfer log showing use for third-party file exchanges in the last quarter
Information transfer policy in Confluence specifying approved channels per classification level with named policy owner and review date
Microsoft Purview message encryption policy configuration showing automatic encryption applied to Confidential-labelled outbound emails
DLP alert log showing detection and blocking of an attempt to send sensitive data via an unapproved channel (e.g., personal email)
Non-disclosure or information transfer agreement template signed by a third party, confirming agreed transfer security requirements

title: Access control
description: Rules to control physical and logical access to information and other associated assets must be established and implemented.
guidance: Implement a role-based access control model reviewed quarterly, with least-privilege as the default.
implementationGuide: Start by documenting your access control policy in Confluence, defining the principles you follow — least privilege, need-to-know, role-based access control (RBAC) — and mapping them to each system in scope. In Okta or Microsoft Entra ID, implement RBAC by defining role groups that align to job functions rather than granting access to individuals directly, making access provisioning consistent and auditable. Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts accessing internal systems, and step-up authentication for privileged access using CyberArk or BeyondTrust PAM. Configure access reviews in Entra ID Access Reviews or Vanta to run quarterly for privileged accounts and semi-annually for standard accounts, producing a report that named managers must certify. The done state is a published access control policy, RBAC enforced in the primary IAM platform, MFA enabled for 100% of users, and quarterly access review reports with named manager sign-offs. Auditors most commonly find failures when access reviews exist on paper but manager certifications are rubber-stamped without genuine review — look for cases where terminated accounts were approved in an access review.
exampleEvidence: Access control policy in Confluence defining RBAC, least privilege, and MFA requirements with named owner and annual review date
Okta or Entra ID RBAC group configuration screenshot showing role-based groups mapped to job functions rather than individuals
MFA enforcement policy screenshot from Okta or Entra ID showing 100% of user accounts required to enrol in MFA
Quarterly access review report from Entra ID Access Reviews or Vanta showing manager certifications with completion date
CyberArk or BeyondTrust PAM session log showing privileged access requests, approvals, and session recordings for the last quarter
Access review exception report showing accounts flagged for removal or modification following the quarterly review cycle

title: Identity management
description: The full lifecycle of identities must be managed to enable their authentication and authorisation to systems and services.
guidance: Implement an IDM system covering joiner/mover/leaver workflows with automated provisioning and de-provisioning.
implementationGuide: Start by centralising all identity management in a single authoritative directory — Microsoft Entra ID or Okta as your Identity Provider (IdP) — and federating all application access through SAML or OIDC SSO so that a single identity record governs access across your estate. Configure automated user lifecycle management: provisioning triggered by your HRIS (BambooHR, Workday) on hire, and deprovisioning within 4 hours of a leaver record being created. Enforce unique identifiers — no shared accounts, no service accounts used by humans — and configure Entra ID or Okta to flag and block duplicate identity creation. Use Privileged Identity Management (PIM) in Entra ID or CyberArk to ensure privileged identities are distinct from regular user identities and require just-in-time activation. The done state is a single IdP governing all application access via SSO, automated provisioning/deprovisioning tied to HRIS events, and zero shared accounts in use. Auditors most commonly find failures where legacy applications are not federated through the IdP and maintain their own local user databases that are not lifecycle-managed.
exampleEvidence: Entra ID or Okta SSO application catalogue screenshot showing all in-scope applications federated through the central IdP
HRIS (BambooHR or Workday) to IdP provisioning workflow diagram or automation log showing triggered deprovisioning within 4 hours of leaver event
Entra ID or Okta user audit report confirming zero shared accounts and all accounts tied to a unique named individual
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) configuration in Entra ID showing just-in-time activation required for all privileged roles
Deprovisioning log extract showing accounts disabled and access revoked within SLA for the last five leavers
Application access matrix showing all systems in scope mapped to the central IdP with federation method (SAML/OIDC) documented

title: Authentication information
description: Allocation and management of authentication information must be controlled by a management process including advising personnel on proper handling.
guidance: Enforce a password policy with minimum length, complexity, and MFA for all privileged and remote accounts.
implementationGuide: Start by enforcing password complexity and rotation requirements through your IdP (Okta or Entra ID) — configure a minimum 12-character password, block commonly breached passwords using Microsoft Entra Password Protection or Okta's breached password detection, and require MFA for all accounts. Eliminate password sharing entirely by auditing for shared credentials in your password manager (1Password Teams, Bitwarden for Business) and revoking any found. For service accounts and API keys, enforce storage in a secrets management vault (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) rather than in code repositories or config files — scan your GitHub or GitLab repos with truffleHog or GitHub secret scanning to detect any committed credentials. Configure your SIEM (Splunk) to alert on authentication anomalies: multiple failed logins, logins from unusual geographies, or logins outside business hours for privileged accounts. The done state is MFA enforced for 100% of user accounts, no shared credentials, all secrets in a vault, and active monitoring for authentication anomalies. Auditors most commonly find failures when service account passwords are stored in plaintext config files or spreadsheets maintained by individual engineers.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Entra ID password policy configuration screenshot showing 12-character minimum, complexity rules, and breached password blocking enabled
MFA enrolment report from Okta or Entra ID showing 100% of user accounts enrolled in MFA with method breakdown
1Password Teams or Bitwarden for Business audit report confirming no shared credentials in use and all vaulted passwords are individual
HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager secrets inventory screenshot showing all service account credentials and API keys stored in vault
GitHub Advanced Security or truffleHog scan report showing zero committed credentials detected in the last 30-day scan
Splunk or Sentinel authentication anomaly alert log showing at least one true-positive alert investigated and resolved in the last quarter

title: Access rights
description: Access rights to information and other associated assets must be provisioned, reviewed, modified, and removed based on defined criteria.
guidance: Conduct quarterly access reviews for all systems holding Confidential or above data; revoke stale access promptly.
implementationGuide: Start by implementing a formal access request and approval workflow — configure this in your ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) so that every access grant requires a business justification, approval from the resource owner, and a defined review date. Apply least privilege at provisioning time: use Entra ID or Okta RBAC groups to grant the minimum set of permissions required for the role, and avoid assigning users to privileged groups directly. Run quarterly access reviews using Entra ID Access Reviews or Vanta, requiring resource owners to certify each account and revoke or modify any that are no longer justified. Automate deprovisioning: configure your HRIS-to-IdP integration (e.g., BambooHR with Okta SCIM) to revoke all access within 4 hours of a role change or termination. The done state is a documented access request workflow with 100% of grants traceable to an approved ticket, quarterly access reviews completed on time, and automated deprovisioning verified in the last test. Auditors most commonly find failures where access accumulated over time through role changes ('permission creep') and was never revoked during access reviews.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow or Jira Service Management access request ticket showing business justification, approver name, and approval date
Entra ID or Okta access review report from last quarter showing resource owner certifications with account-level decisions recorded
HRIS-to-IdP SCIM provisioning log showing access revoked within 4 hours of a leaver record being created in BambooHR or Workday
Access rights register or spreadsheet showing all privileged accounts, their justification, approver, and last review date
Quarterly access review completion certificate signed by the CISO or security manager confirming review was completed within SLA
Audit log from Entra ID or Okta showing accounts disabled or permissions modified as a result of the last quarterly access review

title: Information security in supplier relationships
description: Processes and procedures must be defined and implemented to manage the information security risks associated with the use of supplier products or services.
guidance: Complete a third-party security assessment for all Tier-1 suppliers before contract signing and annually thereafter.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a supplier register that lists every third party with access to your information assets or systems, categorised by risk tier based on data sensitivity and access level — store this in your GRC tool (Vanta, OneTrust) or a managed spreadsheet in Confluence. Require all suppliers to complete a security questionnaire (aligned to SIG Lite or CAIQ) before contract signature, and use the results to drive contract security requirements. Integrate supplier risk into your contract review process: require your legal team to include standard information security clauses in all supplier contracts, covering breach notification, audit rights, subprocessor disclosure, and data return/deletion on termination. Assign an internal relationship owner to each critical supplier responsible for the annual review. The done state is a complete supplier register with risk tiers assigned, security questionnaires on file for all critical suppliers, and security clauses in all active contracts. Auditors most commonly find failures when supplier registers are incomplete — particularly for 'invisible' suppliers like SaaS tools procured by business teams without IT involvement.
exampleEvidence: Supplier register in Vanta or Confluence listing all third-party suppliers with risk tier, data access scope, and named internal owner
Completed SIG Lite or CAIQ security questionnaire for a critical supplier, reviewed and signed off by the security team
Supplier contract extract showing standard information security clauses covering breach notification, audit rights, and data deletion
Annual supplier risk review report showing all critical suppliers reassessed within the last 12 months with findings and actions
OneTrust or manual DPIA for a critical supplier processing personal data, signed by the DPO
Supplier onboarding checklist showing security questionnaire completion as a mandatory gate before contract signature and system access

title: Addressing information security within supplier agreements
description: Relevant information security requirements must be established and agreed with each supplier based on the type of supplier relationship.
guidance: Include a standard information security schedule in all supplier contracts covering data handling, incident notification, and audit rights.
implementationGuide: Start by developing a standard set of information security contract clauses in collaboration with your legal team, covering data classification and handling, incident notification timelines (typically 24-72 hours), audit rights, subprocessor approval, and secure data return/destruction on termination — store these in a clause library in Confluence or your contract management tool (ContractPodAi, Ironclad). Require all new supplier contracts to include these clauses and flag any deviations for CISO approval. For existing contracts, run an annual review to identify gaps and use renewal cycles as an opportunity to insert missing clauses. Configure your contract management tool to alert the supplier relationship owner 90 days before contract renewal so there is time to renegotiate terms. The done state is a clause library in use for all new contracts, a gap analysis completed for all existing contracts, and no critical contracts missing breach notification or data handling clauses. Auditors most commonly find failures when contracts were signed years ago before the current security standards were in place and have never been updated.
exampleEvidence: Standard information security clause library in Confluence or ContractPodAi showing all required clauses with legal review sign-off
Executed supplier contract extract showing information security clauses including breach notification timeline and audit rights
Contract gap analysis report showing all existing supplier contracts reviewed against the clause library with gaps identified and remediation plan
CISO approval record for a clause deviation request submitted by a supplier during contract negotiation
Contract management tool (Ironclad or ContractPodAi) renewal alert configuration showing 90-day advance notification to supplier owners
Supplier agreement amendment or addendum adding information security clauses to a legacy contract at renewal, signed by both parties

title: Managing information security in the ICT supply chain
description: Processes and procedures must be defined and implemented to manage the information security risks associated with the ICT products and services supply chain.
guidance: Maintain a supplier dependency map and conduct software bill of materials (SBOM) reviews for critical ICT components.
implementationGuide: Start by mapping your ICT supply chain: identify all hardware manufacturers, software vendors, cloud providers, and managed service providers that form your technology stack, and document the chain of custody for critical components. For software, implement Software Composition Analysis (SCA) using Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, or GitHub Dependabot to identify open-source components with known vulnerabilities in your applications. For hardware, maintain a hardware bill of materials (HBOM) for critical systems and verify supplier provenance before procurement. Require suppliers of critical ICT components to provide Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for their products, and assess these against known vulnerability databases. Configure your vulnerability management programme (Tenable, Qualys) to include third-party software components in its scope. The done state is an ICT supply chain map, SCA integrated into your CI/CD pipeline, and SBOM received and assessed for all critical software suppliers. Auditors most commonly find failures when the organisation has no visibility into the software components embedded in third-party products — the SolarWinds-style attack vector.
exampleEvidence: ICT supply chain map document showing hardware, software, and service providers with dependency relationships and risk tiers
Snyk or Dependabot SCA scan report integrated into the CI/CD pipeline showing open-source component vulnerabilities for a recent build
SBOM received from a critical software supplier, assessed against the NVD vulnerability database with findings documented
Hardware procurement policy extract requiring supplier provenance verification and approved vendor list sign-off before purchase
Tenable or Qualys scan configuration showing third-party software components included in the vulnerability scanning scope
ICT supply chain risk review meeting minutes showing quarterly assessment of critical supplier security posture with action items

title: Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services
description: The organisation must regularly monitor, review, and manage changes to supplier service delivery, taking account of information security risks.
guidance: Schedule annual service reviews with all Tier-1 suppliers covering SLA performance, incident history, and security posture.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing a formal supplier service review cadence — quarterly for critical suppliers, annually for standard suppliers — with a documented agenda covering security incidents in the period, vulnerability disclosures, patch compliance status, and any changes to their subprocessor or infrastructure. Use your GRC tool (Vanta, OneTrust) to track supplier review dates and generate alerts when reviews are overdue. For cloud and SaaS suppliers, configure alerts in your CASB (Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps) or subscribe to their status page and security advisories. When a supplier notifies you of a significant change (e.g., data centre migration, acquisition, new subprocessor), trigger a re-assessment using your standard security questionnaire. The done state is a supplier review schedule in your GRC tool with completed review records for all critical suppliers in the last 12 months, including a record of any changes assessed. Auditors most commonly find failures when supplier reviews are conducted informally via email with no documented agenda or outputs.
exampleEvidence: Supplier review schedule in Vanta or OneTrust showing review dates, assigned owners, and completion status for all critical suppliers
Quarterly supplier review meeting minutes for a critical supplier covering security incidents, patch status, and change notifications
Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps alert configuration showing monitoring of critical SaaS suppliers for anomalous activity
Supplier change notification log showing a supplier change assessed, risk evaluated, and documented within the defined SLA
GRC tool dashboard screenshot showing supplier review completion rate and overdue review alerts for the current period
Updated security questionnaire assessment for a supplier following a notified significant change, with risk determination documented

title: Information security for use of cloud services
description: Processes for the acquisition, use, management, and exit from cloud services must be established, in accordance with the organisation's information security requirements.
guidance: Define a cloud security baseline policy covering approved services, data residency, encryption, and offboarding procedures.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a cloud security policy that defines approved cloud service providers, classification of data permitted in each, and minimum security baseline requirements (encryption at rest and in transit, MFA, logging). Use a Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool — Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Wiz, or Prisma Cloud — to continuously assess your cloud environment against CIS Benchmarks or your internal baseline and alert on drift. Configure your IdP (Entra ID or Okta) to enforce SSO and MFA for all cloud service access, and use a CASB to monitor and control shadow cloud usage. For multi-cloud environments, implement consistent tagging policies so assets are discoverable and attributed to the correct owner and cost centre. Review cloud provider security documentation (AWS Shared Responsibility Model, Azure Trust Center) and map your responsibilities explicitly. The done state is a cloud security policy in place, CSPM active with documented remediation of all critical findings, and shadow cloud usage monitored via CASB. Auditors most commonly find failures when the organisation assumes the cloud provider is responsible for data protection — the shared responsibility model is frequently misunderstood.
exampleEvidence: Cloud security policy in Confluence listing approved providers, permitted data classifications, and minimum security baseline requirements
Microsoft Defender for Cloud or Wiz CSPM dashboard screenshot showing secure score and list of open critical findings with remediation status
CASB (Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps) shadow IT discovery report showing unapproved cloud services detected and risk-rated
Entra ID or Okta conditional access policy screenshot enforcing MFA and compliant device check for all cloud service access
Cloud provider shared responsibility model mapping document showing which controls are provider-managed vs. customer-managed
Cloud asset tagging compliance report showing 95%+ of cloud resources tagged with owner, environment, and classification labels

title: Information security incident management planning and preparation
description: The organisation must plan and prepare for managing information security incidents by defining, establishing, and communicating processes, roles, and responsibilities.
guidance: Produce and test a formal incident response plan covering detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned.
implementationGuide: Start by drafting an Incident Response Plan (IRP) that defines severity classifications (P1-P4), response timelines, escalation paths, and roles — store it in Confluence and keep a printed copy in a secure location for use during a major outage. Define an Incident Response Team (IRT) with named primary and backup members from security, IT, legal, communications, and senior management. Integrate the IRP with your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) so that high-severity alerts automatically create an incident ticket in Jira or ServiceNow and page the on-call IRT member via PagerDuty or OpsGenie. Conduct at least one tabletop exercise annually using a realistic scenario (ransomware, data breach, insider threat) with documented outputs and action items. The done state is a tested IRP with named IRT members, automated incident ticket creation from SIEM alerts, and documented tabletop exercise results within the last 12 months. Auditors most commonly find failures when the IRP exists but has never been tested — an untested plan is not an effective control.
exampleEvidence: Incident Response Plan document in Confluence defining P1-P4 severity classifications, response timelines, escalation paths, and IRT roles
IRT contact list with named primary and backup members from security, IT, legal, and communications, reviewed within 6 months
Splunk or Sentinel alert-to-ticket automation configuration showing automatic Jira/ServiceNow ticket creation for P1/P2 severity alerts
PagerDuty or OpsGenie on-call schedule screenshot showing IRT rotation covering 24/7 incident response capability
Tabletop exercise report from the last 12 months showing scenario, participants, findings, and action items with owners and due dates
Incident classification matrix document showing decision tree for assigning severity levels with worked examples for each tier

title: Assessment and decision on information security events
description: The organisation must assess information security events and decide if they are to be categorised as information security incidents.
guidance: Create a severity classification matrix and train the SOC/service desk to triage events within a defined time window.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a clear triage process for all security events reported through your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel), helpdesk (ServiceNow), or user reporting channels — every event should receive an initial assessment within 30 minutes of detection during business hours. Create a decision framework (flowchart or decision tree) that classifies events as false positive, security event, or security incident based on observable indicators, and publish it in Confluence for all security analysts to use. Configure your SIEM to automatically enrich alerts with asset owner, classification, and threat intelligence context from Recorded Future or MISP so analysts have the information needed to make rapid decisions. Track all events in your SIEM or ticketing system with timestamps for detection, triage decision, and escalation. The done state is a documented triage process, a decision framework in use by all analysts, and SIEM alert enrichment configured — with average triage time under 30 minutes for P1 events evidenced by ticket timestamps. Auditors most commonly find failures when events are triaged informally with no documented decision or rationale, making it impossible to demonstrate the assessment process.
exampleEvidence: Event triage decision framework (flowchart) published in Confluence showing classification criteria for false positive, event, and incident
Splunk or Sentinel alert enrichment configuration screenshot showing automatic context addition (asset owner, classification, threat intel) to alerts
Incident ticket in ServiceNow or Jira showing timestamps for event detection, triage decision, classification, and escalation decision
SIEM dashboard showing mean time to triage (MTTT) metric for P1 events over the last quarter, demonstrating sub-30-minute performance
Security operations runbook for a specific alert type (e.g., malware detection) showing triage steps and decision criteria
Monthly security event summary report showing total events triaged, classification breakdown, and escalation rate trends

title: Response to information security incidents
description: Information security incidents must be responded to in accordance with the documented procedures.
guidance: Assign an incident commander role and conduct quarterly tabletop exercises to rehearse the response playbook.
implementationGuide: Start by defining response playbooks for your top five most likely incident types — ransomware, data breach, phishing, insider threat, DDoS — each stored in Confluence with step-by-step containment, eradication, and recovery actions. Assign a named Incident Commander for each incident who is responsible for coordinating the response, maintaining an incident timeline log, and making key decisions such as isolation actions. Integrate your response tooling: your EDR (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) should allow remote isolation of affected endpoints, your SIEM should be used for forensic log analysis, and your communication platform (Slack or Teams) should have a dedicated incident channel created automatically by your SOAR tool (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR). Engage legal counsel and your communications team for any incident likely to require external notification. The done state is a set of tested playbooks covering top incident types, a documented response log for every P1/P2 incident in the last 12 months, and evidence of containment actions taken. Auditors most commonly find failures when incident timelines are not maintained during the incident, making it impossible to reconstruct events for the post-incident review.
exampleEvidence: Incident response playbook for ransomware in Confluence showing step-by-step containment, eradication, and recovery actions with named role owners
P1 incident timeline log from a real or simulated incident showing timestamps for detection, containment, eradication, and recovery milestones
CrowdStrike Falcon or Defender for Endpoint remote isolation action log showing endpoint isolated during an incident response
Splunk SOAR or XSOAR automated response workflow screenshot showing playbook execution log for a triggered incident
Legal counsel and communications team engagement record (email or ticket) for an incident requiring external notification
Post-incident review report documenting root cause, impact assessment, lessons learned, and remediation actions with due dates

title: Learning from information security incidents
description: Knowledge gained from information security incidents must be used to strengthen and improve controls.
guidance: Produce and distribute a post-incident review report within 10 working days of closure for all P1/P2 incidents.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing a mandatory post-incident review (PIR) process for all P1 and P2 incidents — the PIR must be completed within 5 business days of incident closure and must follow a structured template covering timeline, root cause analysis, impact assessment, lessons learned, and remediation actions with owners and due dates. Store all PIRs in a dedicated Confluence space and track remediation actions in Jira with named owners and deadlines. Conduct a quarterly incident trend analysis to identify patterns — recurring alert types, repeated root causes, or systemic control gaps — and present findings to the security steering committee. Feed lessons learned into your training programme (KnowBe4) and your runbooks so improvements are embedded in future response capability. The done state is a PIR on file for every P1/P2 incident in the last 12 months, a remediation action tracker with closed items evidenced, and a quarterly trend analysis presented to management. Auditors most commonly find failures when PIRs are started but not completed, or remediation actions are tracked nowhere and fall through the cracks.
exampleEvidence: Completed post-incident review report for a P1 incident showing timeline, root cause analysis, lessons learned, and remediation actions with owners
Jira remediation action tracker showing items raised from PIRs with status (open/closed), assignee, and due date for the last four quarters
Quarterly incident trend analysis report presented to the security steering committee showing pattern analysis and recommended control improvements
KnowBe4 or Mimecast training module update record showing a lesson learned from an incident incorporated into the phishing awareness programme
PIR Confluence space index showing a PIR document for every P1/P2 incident in the last 12 months with completion date and status
Security steering committee minutes referencing the quarterly incident trend analysis with management decision on remediation prioritisation

title: Collection of evidence
description: The organisation must establish and implement procedures for the identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of evidence related to information security events.
guidance: Ensure log retention meets regulatory minimums and document chain-of-custody procedures for forensic investigations.
implementationGuide: Start by defining an evidence handling procedure that covers identification, collection, preservation, chain of custody, and storage — ensure this is drafted in consultation with legal counsel and stored in Confluence so it is accessible during an incident when time pressure is high. Configure your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) with log retention of at least 12 months (hot) and 7 years (cold archive) to ensure forensic logs are available if an investigation arises months after an event. For endpoint forensics, ensure your EDR (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) is configured to retain process execution, network connection, and file creation telemetry at sufficient depth. When collecting evidence, use write-blocker tools and hash verification (SHA-256) to demonstrate forensic integrity — document the hash in the chain of custody record. Engage a certified digital forensics provider (e.g., Stroz Friedberg, Kroll) for major incidents requiring legal defensibility. The done state is a documented evidence handling procedure, SIEM log retention configured to policy, and chain of custody records for any evidence collected in the last 12 months. Auditors most commonly find failures when log retention is insufficient — logs needed for a forensic investigation have already been purged.
exampleEvidence: Evidence handling procedure document in Confluence covering identification, collection, preservation, chain of custody, and storage requirements
Splunk or Sentinel log retention configuration screenshot showing 12-month hot retention and 7-year cold archive policy
Chain of custody form completed for evidence collected during an incident investigation, including SHA-256 hash verification
CrowdStrike Falcon or Defender for Endpoint telemetry retention configuration showing process, network, and file telemetry retention depth
Digital forensics engagement letter from a certified provider (e.g., Kroll) for a major incident requiring legal defensibility
Legal counsel sign-off on evidence handling procedure confirming alignment with local evidence admissibility requirements

title: Information security during disruption
description: The organisation must plan how to maintain information security at an appropriate level during disruption.
guidance: Include information security requirements (e.g. access controls, encryption) in BCP and DR plans and test them annually.
implementationGuide: Start by identifying your most critical information processing activities and mapping them to specific IT systems — this is the foundation for your Business Continuity Plan (BCP). For each critical system, define a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO), and ensure your backup and recovery architecture can meet these targets. Use your backup platform (Veeam, Azure Backup, AWS Backup) to configure automated backups aligned to RPO targets, and test restores quarterly to validate that backups are usable. Integrate information security requirements into your BCP so that security controls are maintained during disruption — for example, ensure that failover systems have the same access controls and logging as production. The done state is a tested BCP with documented RTO/RPO targets, quarterly backup restore test results, and evidence that security controls are maintained in the continuity plan. Auditors most commonly find failures when BCPs are written but backups are never tested — discovering that backups are corrupt during a real incident is a catastrophic failure.
exampleEvidence: Business Continuity Plan document showing critical systems, RTO/RPO targets, recovery procedures, and named recovery team members
Veeam or Azure Backup configuration screenshot showing automated backup schedules aligned to documented RPO targets
Quarterly backup restore test report showing specific files/systems restored, test result (success/failure), and tested-by name
BCP test exercise report from the last 12 months documenting scenario, participants, outcomes, and action items
Failover environment access control configuration showing same RBAC and logging requirements as production environment
RTO/RPO validation report demonstrating that recovery time achieved during testing meets the documented business requirement

title: ICT readiness for business continuity
description: ICT readiness must be planned, implemented, maintained, and tested based on business continuity objectives and ICT continuity requirements.
guidance: Define RTO/RPO for each critical system and validate them through annual failover tests.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a Business Impact Analysis (BIA) that identifies maximum tolerable downtime (MTD) for each critical ICT service and maps it to explicit RTO and RPO targets — engage business process owners to validate the targets reflect real operational needs. Design your ICT architecture for resilience: use multi-availability-zone deployments in AWS or Azure, configure load balancing and auto-scaling for critical workloads, and use Azure Site Recovery or AWS Disaster Recovery service for failover of on-premises workloads. Test your ICT continuity arrangements at least annually using a realistic failover scenario — not just a backup restore test but a full failover to the secondary environment with measured RTO. Document the test, findings, and remediation actions. The done state is a BIA with validated RTO/RPO, architecture diagrams showing resilience design, and an annual failover test report with measured recovery times. Auditors most commonly find failures when the architecture diagram shows redundancy but the failover has never been tested, so it is unknown whether it actually works.
exampleEvidence: Business Impact Analysis document showing MTD, RTO, and RPO for each critical ICT service, validated and signed by business process owners
AWS or Azure architecture diagram showing multi-AZ deployment, load balancing, and auto-scaling configuration for critical workloads
Azure Site Recovery or AWS DR configuration showing replication status and last successful replication timestamp for critical systems
Annual ICT continuity test report showing full failover to secondary environment, measured RTO achieved, and comparison to target RTO
Load balancing and auto-scaling configuration screenshot showing health check thresholds and scale-out policies for critical applications
Post-test remediation action tracker showing findings from the annual test and status of corrective actions with due dates

title: Legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements
description: Legal, statutory, regulatory, and contractual requirements relevant to information security and the organisation's approach to meeting these requirements must be identified, documented, and kept up to date.
guidance: Maintain a legal and regulatory obligations register reviewed quarterly by the DPO or legal team.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a legal landscape review for every jurisdiction in which you operate — engage legal counsel to produce a requirements register covering data protection law (GDPR, UK GDPR, CCPA), sector regulation (PCI-DSS, FCA, HIPAA), employment law, and intellectual property. Store this register in Confluence and assign an owner responsible for monitoring regulatory changes (subscribe to ICO, FCA, and sector regulator mailing lists). Map each legal requirement to the specific technical and organisational controls that satisfy it, so you have a clear line of sight from regulation to implementation. Review the requirements register at least annually and whenever a significant regulatory change is announced. The done state is a maintained requirements register with controls mapped, reviewed within 12 months, and evidence of monitoring for regulatory changes. Auditors most commonly find failures when organisations operate in multiple jurisdictions but have only mapped requirements for their home country, missing obligations in other markets.
exampleEvidence: Legal and regulatory requirements register in Confluence covering all applicable jurisdictions with control mappings and named owner
ICO registration certificate and GDPR Article 30 records of processing activities document for UK operations
Legal counsel sign-off on the requirements register confirming completeness for all operating jurisdictions, dated within 12 months
Regulatory change monitoring log showing subscription to ICO, FCA, and sector regulator updates with alerts reviewed and actioned
Control mapping document showing each legal requirement mapped to specific ISO 27001 controls satisfying the obligation
Annual requirements register review sign-off with changelog showing updates made in response to regulatory changes in the period

title: Intellectual property rights
description: The organisation must implement appropriate procedures to protect intellectual property rights.
guidance: Document software licence management procedures and conduct an annual audit of installed versus licensed software.
implementationGuide: Start by creating an Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) policy that covers software licensing compliance, copyright, patent obligations, and the use of open-source software — publish it in Confluence with clear guidance for engineers, procurement, and marketing teams. Deploy a Software Asset Management (SAM) tool (Flexera, Snow Software) to maintain a complete inventory of all software licences, map them to deployed installations, and alert on over-deployment or under-licensing. For open-source, implement a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool (Snyk, FOSSA) in your CI/CD pipeline to identify open-source licences and flag any copyleft licences (GPL, AGPL) that may conflict with your commercial software distribution. Run an annual software licence audit and reconcile against the SAM tool inventory. The done state is a published IPR policy, SAM tool in use with zero over-deployed licences, and SCA integrated into the development pipeline. Auditors most commonly find failures when engineers use open-source components without checking licence obligations, resulting in undisclosed GPL code in commercial products.
exampleEvidence: Intellectual Property Rights policy in Confluence covering software licensing, copyright, and open-source obligations with named owner
Flexera or Snow Software SAM dashboard showing all licensed software, installation counts, and licence compliance status
Snyk or FOSSA SCA pipeline integration screenshot showing open-source licence detection results for a recent build
Annual software licence audit report reconciling SAM inventory against vendor invoices and true-up obligations
Open-source licence risk report flagging copyleft licences (GPL/AGPL) detected in the codebase with remediation decisions
Software procurement policy extract requiring licence verification and SAM registration before any software purchase is approved

title: Protection of records
description: Records must be protected from loss, destruction, falsification, unauthorised access, and unauthorised release.
guidance: Define a records retention schedule aligned to legal requirements and implement access controls and backup procedures.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a records management policy that defines retention periods for each record type — financial records (7 years), HR records (6 years post-employment), security logs (1-7 years depending on type), and contracts — aligned to your legal jurisdiction requirements. Map all record storage locations (SharePoint, S3, on-premises file server) and configure retention labels in Microsoft Purview or your document management system to enforce automatic deletion or archival at the end of the retention period. Protect records from tampering by enabling object lock or immutable storage in AWS S3 or Azure Blob Storage for logs and audit trails. Restrict deletion of records during their retention period — only a records manager with explicit approval should be able to delete records early. The done state is a records retention schedule enforced technically through your document management and cloud storage platforms, with immutable logging enabled for security logs. Auditors most commonly find failures when records are stored in SharePoint with no retention policy, meaning they can be deleted at any time or are never purged, creating a data minimisation compliance risk.
exampleEvidence: Records retention schedule document showing record types, retention periods, storage locations, and disposal methods, reviewed within 12 months
Microsoft Purview retention label configuration showing labels applied to SharePoint libraries enforcing defined retention periods
AWS S3 Object Lock or Azure Blob immutable storage configuration showing WORM policy applied to security log storage buckets
Records management policy in Confluence with legal counsel sign-off confirming retention periods are compliant with applicable law
Purview records management report showing percentage of documents with applied retention labels and items due for deletion review
Secure disposal certificate from a records destruction provider confirming physical records destroyed in accordance with the retention schedule

title: Privacy and protection of personal information
description: The organisation must identify and meet requirements for the preservation of privacy and protection of personal information as required by applicable legislation and regulations.
guidance: Conduct a DPIA for all new processing activities involving personal data and maintain a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA).
implementationGuide: Start by appointing a Data Protection Officer (DPO) or privacy lead and ensuring they have a direct reporting line to senior management with authority to act independently. Build your privacy programme on your Article 30 Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) — maintain this in OneTrust or a structured Confluence template, ensuring every processing activity is documented with legal basis, data categories, retention period, and transfer details. Implement Privacy by Design principles in your development lifecycle: embed a DPIA trigger checklist in your project intake process (Jira) so that any project involving personal data triggers a DPIA before design begins. Configure Microsoft Purview or your DLP tool to detect and prevent unauthorised sharing of PII. Conduct annual privacy training through your LMS (KnowBe4) with role-specific modules for high-risk functions like HR and marketing. The done state is a maintained RoPA, DPIAs completed for all high-risk processing activities, DLP configured for PII, and >90% of staff trained on privacy. Auditors most commonly find failures when the RoPA exists but is outdated, missing processing activities added in the last 12 months.
exampleEvidence: Article 30 RoPA document maintained in OneTrust or Confluence showing all processing activities with legal basis, data categories, and retention periods
DPO appointment letter or job description confirming independent reporting line to senior management
Completed DPIA for a high-risk processing activity, reviewed and signed off by the DPO with residual risk determination
Microsoft Purview DLP policy configuration showing PII detection rules and blocking actions for external sharing
KnowBe4 privacy training completion report showing >90% staff completion rate with role-specific module differentiation
RoPA review sign-off confirming all processing activities reviewed and updated within the last 12 months, dated and signed by the DPO

title: Independent review of information security
description: The organisation's approach to managing information security and its implementation must be reviewed independently at planned intervals.
guidance: Commission an independent internal or external audit of the ISMS at least annually and present findings to senior management.
implementationGuide: Start by scheduling an independent review of your ISMS at least annually — this can be an internal audit conducted by someone independent of the security function, or a third-party audit by a CREST-certified consultancy or your certification body's surveillance audit. Define the audit scope, objectives, and criteria in an audit plan published in Confluence, and ensure the auditor has unrestricted access to documentation, systems, and key personnel. Produce a formal audit report with findings categorised by severity (major nonconformity, minor nonconformity, observation) and track remediation actions in Jira with named owners and deadlines. Present the audit report to the security steering committee and board. The done state is an audit report from an independent party in the last 12 months, a remediation action tracker showing all major findings closed, and evidence of board-level visibility. Auditors most commonly find failures when internal audits are conducted by the same team responsible for implementing controls — true independence requires a different function or an external party.
exampleEvidence: Independent ISMS audit report from a CREST-certified consultancy or internal audit function, dated within the last 12 months
Audit plan document showing scope, objectives, methodology, and auditor independence declaration
Remediation action tracker in Jira showing all major nonconformities from the audit with status (open/closed) and target dates
Security steering committee or board minutes showing the audit report presented and discussed, with management response recorded
