Evidence of auditor independence — either engagement letter from a third-party firm or internal audit charter confirming separation from the security function
Closed finding evidence pack showing before/after documentation for remediated audit nonconformities

title: Compliance with policies, rules and standards for information security
description: Compliance with the organisation's information security policy, topic-specific policies, rules, and standards must be regularly reviewed.
guidance: Run quarterly compliance self-assessments at department level and escalate non-conformities to the CISO.
implementationGuide: Start by building a compliance monitoring programme that reviews adherence to your information security policies on a regular cycle — use your GRC tool (Vanta, Drata) to run automated compliance checks against your technical controls (MFA, patch compliance, endpoint encryption) and flag deviations in real time. For policy-based controls that cannot be automated, schedule manual compliance reviews on a quarterly basis and document results in Confluence. Configure your SIEM (Splunk) to alert on policy violations — for example, USB device connection attempts blocked by endpoint policy, or login attempts from blocked countries. Produce a monthly compliance dashboard for the security steering committee showing control compliance rates and open exceptions. The done state is an automated compliance monitoring programme covering all technical controls, a manual review cycle for non-automatable controls, and monthly compliance reporting to management. Auditors most commonly find failures when the compliance monitoring programme reports green status but has not detected genuine deviations — they will test controls directly to verify.
exampleEvidence: Vanta or Drata automated compliance dashboard screenshot showing control compliance rates for all in-scope technical controls
Splunk or Sentinel policy violation alert log showing detected and investigated violations in the last quarter
Quarterly manual compliance review report for non-automatable controls with reviewer name, findings, and remediation actions
Monthly compliance dashboard report presented to the security steering committee with trend data over the last six months
Policy exception register showing all approved exceptions with justification, compensating control, and expiry date
Compliance monitoring programme procedure document defining automated checks, manual review cadence, and escalation path for violations

title: Documented operating procedures
description: Operating procedures for information processing facilities must be documented and made available to personnel who need them.
guidance: Store all operating procedures in a version-controlled location with a named owner per procedure.
implementationGuide: Start by creating an operating procedure register in Confluence that lists every documented procedure required for your critical IT and security processes — system startup/shutdown, backup, patch management, incident response, access provisioning, and change management. Each procedure must be stored in a version-controlled Confluence page with a named owner, last review date, and step-by-step instructions written at a level that allows a qualified substitute to execute them without prior knowledge. Assign a procedure review owner to each document and schedule annual reviews — use Confluence page reminders or Jira tasks to track upcoming reviews. Ensure procedures are accessible to the team 24/7, including during major incidents when the primary document author may be unavailable (consider a read-only printed copy in the server room for network outage scenarios). The done state is a procedure register with all critical processes documented, 100% of procedures reviewed within 12 months, and named owners for each. Auditors most commonly find failures when procedures exist for some processes but critical ones (like backup restoration) are undocumented, relying entirely on tribal knowledge.
exampleEvidence: Procedure register in Confluence listing all critical IT and security procedures with named owner, version, and last review date
Sample operating procedure document (e.g., backup restoration procedure) with step-by-step instructions and version history
Confluence page review history showing annual review completed by named owner for each procedure within the last 12 months
Jira or Confluence task list showing upcoming procedure reviews scheduled with assigned owners and due dates
Procedure gap analysis report identifying any critical processes lacking documented procedures with remediation plan and deadline
IT operations team confirmation that all critical procedures are accessible during network outage scenarios (e.g., printed copies or offline access)

title: Screening
description: Background verification checks on all candidates for employment must be carried out prior to joining the organisation, in accordance with relevant laws, regulations, and ethical considerations.
guidance: Define background check requirements by role sensitivity and integrate the process with HR onboarding workflows.
implementationGuide: Start by tiering your screening requirements based on role sensitivity — define in a screening policy which checks are required for each tier (standard, elevated, privileged) and document this in Confluence. Standard roles should include identity verification, right-to-work confirmation, and employment history checks; privileged roles (IT admin, finance, executive) should additionally include criminal record checks and credit history checks where legally permissible under local employment law. Partner with a specialist screening provider such as HireRight, Experian, or Disclosure Scotland and configure their API or workflow to integrate with your HRIS (BambooHR, Workday) so that screening is automatically triggered when an offer letter is issued. Define a policy for adverse result handling — document the decision criteria, require HR and legal sign-off, and ensure the policy is applied consistently. Make screening completion a mandatory gate in your HRIS before IT access provisioning is triggered through your IdP (Okta or Entra ID). The done state is a tiered screening policy, all hires in the last 12 months with completed screening records in the HRIS, and a documented adverse result handling procedure. Auditors most commonly find failures where screening was completed but results were not formally recorded in the HRIS, or where privileged roles received only standard-tier checks.
exampleEvidence: Screening policy in Confluence defining check types by role tier with legal basis for each check type and named policy owner
HireRight or Experian screening completion report for all hires in the last 12 months, showing check type, result, and completion date
HRIS (BambooHR or Workday) onboarding workflow screenshot showing screening completion as a mandatory gate before IT access provisioning
Adverse result handling procedure document reviewed by HR and legal counsel, with documented decision criteria and sign-off
Sample completed screening record for a privileged role hire showing all required checks completed before system access was granted
HRIS integration configuration showing automated screening trigger linked to offer letter issuance in the recruitment workflow

title: Terms and conditions of employment
description: Employment contractual agreements must state the personnel's and the organisation's responsibilities for information security.
guidance: Include an information security obligations clause in all contracts and the employee handbook.
implementationGuide: Start by ensuring every employment contract and contractor agreement contains explicit information security obligations — data confidentiality, acceptable use of systems, incident reporting duties, and consequences of non-compliance. Work with legal counsel to create standard security clauses for your employment contract template stored in your contract management system (Ironclad, ContractPodAi) or HRIS. For existing employees, issue a security addendum or ensure the annual policy acknowledgement process captures these obligations explicitly. Configure your HRIS (BambooHR, Workday) to record the signing of security terms as a required onboarding step and report on completion. For contractors and third-party workers, ensure equivalent terms are in their service agreements and personal data handling addenda. The done state is a standard employment contract template with security clauses, 100% of current employees with signed terms on record in the HRIS, and equivalent provisions in all contractor agreements. Auditors most commonly find failures where contractors are onboarded without security-specific terms because the procurement process uses a different contract template that legal has not updated.
exampleEvidence: Standard employment contract template extract showing information security obligations, confidentiality clause, and AUP reference
HRIS (BambooHR or Workday) report showing 100% of current employees with signed employment agreements including security terms on file
Contractor service agreement template extract showing equivalent security obligations and data handling requirements
Legal counsel sign-off on employment contract security clauses confirming compliance with local employment law
Onboarding workflow screenshot in HRIS showing security terms sign-off as a mandatory step before day-one access is granted
Annual policy acknowledgement completion report from Vanta or HRIS showing all employees acknowledged current security obligations within 12 months

title: Information security awareness, education and training
description: Personnel of the organisation and relevant interested parties must receive appropriate awareness education and training, and regular updates to the organisation's information security policy.
guidance: Deploy a mandatory annual security awareness programme with phishing simulations and track completion in an LMS.
implementationGuide: Start by building a security awareness training programme in your LMS (KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness) with a mandatory annual baseline course for all staff and role-specific modules for high-risk functions (IT, finance, HR, executives). Configure your LMS to automatically enrol new hires in the baseline course within their first week and send escalating reminders until completion. Run monthly phishing simulations using KnowBe4 or Proofpoint, targeting all staff, and track click rates, credential submission rates, and report rates — use this data to identify individuals or departments needing additional training. Present training completion rates and phishing simulation results to the security steering committee quarterly. Supplement formal training with a security culture programme: a monthly security newsletter, Slack/Teams security tips channel, and participation in Cyber Security Awareness Month (October). The done state is a documented training programme with >95% annual completion, monthly phishing simulations with improving click rates, and role-specific training for high-risk functions. Auditors most commonly find failures where training completion is high but phishing simulation click rates remain flat — high completion does not guarantee behaviour change.
exampleEvidence: KnowBe4 or Proofpoint LMS training completion report showing >95% of all staff completed annual security awareness training within the last 12 months
Monthly phishing simulation results dashboard showing click rate, credential submission rate, and report rate trends over the last 12 months
Role-specific training completion report showing IT admin, finance, and executive teams completed elevated security awareness modules
New hire training enrolment automation confirmation showing baseline security course assigned within first 5 days of employment
Security steering committee deck extract showing training metrics and phishing simulation results presented in the last quarter
Security newsletter or intranet post from the last month demonstrating ongoing security culture programme beyond the annual compliance training

title: Disciplinary process
description: A disciplinary process must be in place and communicated to take action against personnel who have committed an information security policy violation.
guidance: Document a graduated disciplinary procedure for security breaches and ensure HR and legal teams are aligned.
implementationGuide: Start by documenting a disciplinary procedure in your HR policies (stored in Confluence or your HRIS) that explicitly includes information security violations as grounds for disciplinary action, up to and including termination for serious breaches. Work with HR and legal to categorise security violations by severity — minor (e.g., failing to lock screen), moderate (e.g., sharing credentials), and gross misconduct (e.g., data exfiltration, deliberate sabotage) — and map each to the appropriate disciplinary step. Ensure managers are trained on how to escalate suspected security violations to HR and the security team simultaneously. Configure your DLP tool (Microsoft Purview) and UEBA/SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) to generate alerts when data exfiltration indicators are detected, feeding directly into the investigation process. All disciplinary proceedings involving information security must be documented and retained by HR. The done state is a published disciplinary procedure referencing security violations, evidence that it has been applied consistently in the last 12 months, and integration between security tooling and HR escalation. Auditors most commonly find failures when the disciplinary procedure exists but security violations have never been formally processed through it — evidence of application is required, not just the existence of the procedure.
exampleEvidence: HR disciplinary procedure document in Confluence explicitly referencing information security violations with severity categories and corresponding disciplinary steps
Microsoft Purview DLP alert log showing a detected policy violation escalated to HR and security in accordance with the procedure
Anonymised disciplinary case summary showing a security violation was processed through the formal procedure within the last 12 months
Manager training completion record showing all line managers completed training on escalating security violations to HR
Splunk or Sentinel UEBA alert configuration showing data exfiltration detection rules feeding the security violation investigation workflow
Legal counsel review sign-off on the disciplinary procedure confirming compatibility with local employment law

title: Responsibilities after termination or change of employment
description: Information security responsibilities and duties that remain valid after termination or change of employment must be defined, enforced, and communicated.
guidance: Include post-employment obligations (confidentiality, IP ownership) in exit interview and off-boarding documentation.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a formal offboarding procedure that is triggered immediately upon receipt of resignation or termination notice — the procedure must cover access revocation, asset return, data transfer, and completion of confidentiality obligations. Configure your HRIS (BambooHR, Workday) to trigger an automated offboarding workflow in your ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira) that tasks IT to disable all accounts within 4 hours of the leaver's last day, and in cases of involuntary termination, within 1 hour. Use Okta Lifecycle Management or Entra ID automated provisioning to deactivate the leaver's identity, revoking access to all federated applications simultaneously. Conduct an exit interview or security debrief for privileged role leavers to remind them of continuing confidentiality obligations. Review any NDAs or confidentiality agreements to ensure they cover post-employment obligations explicitly. The done state is a documented offboarding procedure with automated access revocation within SLA, evidence of execution for all leavers in the last 12 months, and NDA/confidentiality reminders for privileged leavers. Auditors most commonly find failures where access was revoked from the corporate directory but orphaned accounts in non-federated applications (legacy systems, SaaS tools) remained active.
exampleEvidence: HRIS offboarding workflow screenshot showing automated ServiceNow/Jira task creation for account deactivation within 4-hour SLA
Okta or Entra ID account deactivation log showing all application access revoked within SLA for a sample of recent leavers
Offboarding checklist for a recent leaver showing all steps completed including non-federated application account review
Exit interview or security debrief record for a privileged role leaver confirming post-employment confidentiality obligations communicated
NDA or confidentiality agreement extract showing post-employment obligations clause, signed by the employee
Access review report run 30 days after a leaver's departure confirming zero residual active accounts attributed to the former employee

title: Confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements
description: Confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements reflecting the organisation's needs for the protection of information must be identified, documented, regularly reviewed, and signed by personnel and other relevant interested parties.
guidance: Require NDA signature at hiring and at each contract renewal; review NDA templates at least every two years.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a standard NDA and confidentiality agreement template in consultation with legal counsel, covering both employees and contractors — store it in your contract management system (Ironclad, ContractPodAi) or as a Confluence template. Ensure the NDA covers: scope of confidential information (aligned to your classification scheme), obligations during and post-employment, permitted disclosures, and consequences of breach. Configure your HRIS to require NDA signing as a mandatory onboarding step before any access is granted. For third-party contractors and visitors who will be exposed to confidential information, require NDA signature before any discussion of sensitive matters — track these in a visitor/contractor NDA register. Review and update the NDA template annually with legal counsel, particularly following significant changes in data protection law or your business activities. The done state is a standard NDA template in legal-reviewed use, 100% of current staff and contractors with signed NDAs on file, and a visitor NDA register maintained. Auditors most commonly find failures where contractor NDAs are managed separately from the main HR process and are incomplete or expired.
exampleEvidence: Standard NDA template with legal counsel review sign-off showing scope, duration of obligations, and consequence of breach clauses
HRIS report showing 100% of current employees with signed NDA on file as part of their onboarding record
Contractor/third-party NDA register showing all active contractors with signed NDA, signing date, and expiry date
Visitor NDA log showing signed agreements for all visitors exposed to confidential information in the last quarter
Annual NDA template review sign-off from legal counsel confirming template remains current and legally sound
HRIS onboarding workflow screenshot showing NDA signing as a mandatory step before IT access provisioning is triggered

title: Remote working
description: Security measures must be implemented when personnel are working remotely to protect information accessed, processed, or stored outside the organisation's premises.
guidance: Enforce VPN or zero-trust network access, MDM on remote devices, and a documented remote working security policy.
implementationGuide: Start by publishing a remote working policy in Confluence that defines security requirements for remote workers: approved devices (corporate-managed only unless BYOD policy applies), required VPN or Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) connection, screen privacy in public spaces, and prohibition on accessing sensitive data over public Wi-Fi without encryption. Enforce remote access security technically: configure Microsoft Intune or Jamf to apply device compliance policies (disk encryption, screen lock, MDM enrolment) before granting access through Entra ID Conditional Access. Deploy a ZTNA solution (Zscaler, Cloudflare Access) or enforce VPN with split-tunnelling restrictions to ensure remote workers route corporate traffic securely. For home office setups, provide a security checklist covering router password changes and WPA3 Wi-Fi configuration. Run remote working-specific phishing simulations to test awareness of home-based attack scenarios. The done state is a published remote working policy, Conditional Access enforcing device compliance for all remote access, and a ZTNA or VPN solution in use. Auditors most commonly find failures where BYOD devices are used for corporate access without any MDM enrolment or compliance checking.
exampleEvidence: Remote working policy in Confluence defining device requirements, network security obligations, and prohibited activities with named owner
Entra ID Conditional Access policy screenshot showing device compliance requirement enforced before granting access to corporate applications
Microsoft Intune or Jamf device compliance policy configuration showing disk encryption, screen lock, and MDM enrolment requirements
Zscaler or Cloudflare Access ZTNA configuration showing all remote corporate traffic routed through the secure access gateway
Remote working security checklist distributed to all remote workers showing home network configuration requirements
KnowBe4 remote working phishing simulation results from the last quarter showing click rate and training completion for simulated home-working attacks

title: Information security event reporting
description: Personnel must be able to report observed or suspected information security events through appropriate channels in a timely manner.
guidance: Promote a clear, simple reporting channel (e.g. security@company.com or a helpdesk form) and confirm receipt within 24 hours.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a simple, frictionless security event reporting mechanism — a dedicated email alias (security@yourcompany.com), a Slack/Teams channel, and a button in your ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — and publicise all channels to all staff. Train staff on what constitutes a reportable event (suspicious email, unexpected password reset, lost device, unusual system behaviour) through your annual security awareness programme (KnowBe4) and embed the reporting contact in the AUP. Configure your SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) to ingest reports from the email alias automatically and create incidents. Measure and reward reporting behaviour: track the number of user-reported events monthly and celebrate high reporters — a reporting culture is a leading indicator of security maturity. Guarantee a 'no blame' policy for good-faith reporting to remove the fear of punishment from reporters who made an honest mistake. The done state is at least three reporting channels publicised to all staff, a documented triage process for received reports, and measurable evidence of reporting volume over the last 12 months. Auditors most commonly find failures where the only reporting mechanism is an obscure email address that no one knows about — effective reporting requires active promotion and reinforcement.
exampleEvidence: Security reporting channels documented in the AUP and onboarding materials showing email alias, Slack/Teams channel, and ITSM portal link
Jira Service Management or ServiceNow security event report form screenshot showing mandatory fields and submission confirmation
Monthly security event report volume dashboard showing trend of user-reported events over the last 12 months
KnowBe4 or LMS security awareness training extract showing what-to-report guidance and reporting channel details
SIEM (Splunk or Sentinel) email alias ingestion configuration showing automatic incident creation from security@ mailbox
No-blame policy statement included in the ISMS or AUP confirming good-faith reporters are protected from disciplinary action

title: Physical security perimeters
description: Security perimeters must be defined and used to protect areas that contain information and other associated assets.
guidance: Define secure perimeter zones (reception, general office, server room) with documented access controls for each.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a physical security perimeter assessment of all facilities where information assets are processed or stored — identify all entry points, define the boundary of each secure zone, and document the controls at each boundary. Define physical security zones based on data sensitivity: public zone (reception), general office (internal), restricted zone (server room, finance, HR), and highly restricted (data centre, comms room). Install physical barriers appropriate to each zone — badge readers, mantrap entries, security glass, or turnstiles — and configure your Physical Access Control System (PACS) such as Lenel, Honeywell Pro-Watch, or Genetec to grant access only to authorised role holders. Commission a physical penetration test annually by a CREST-certified physical security firm to test perimeter effectiveness. The done state is a documented perimeter map with zone definitions, PACS configured to enforce zone access by role, and an annual physical pen test report with remediated findings. Auditors most commonly find failures where the server room door is secured with a badge reader but the door itself does not close fully or can be tailgated easily.
exampleEvidence: Physical security perimeter map showing all facilities, zone boundaries, entry points, and access control mechanism at each boundary
PACS (Lenel, Genetec, or Honeywell) access group configuration showing role-based access assignments for restricted and highly restricted zones
CREST-certified physical penetration test report from the last 12 months showing findings and remediation status
Physical security risk assessment document showing threat identification, perimeter controls evaluated, and residual risk accepted
Badge reader installation photographs with timestamps for each secure zone entry point
Access log extract from PACS showing entry events for a restricted zone with role-based access enforced over a 30-day period

title: Physical entry controls
description: Secure areas must be protected by appropriate entry controls and access points.
guidance: Deploy badge readers or PIN-controlled access for all secure areas and review access logs monthly.
implementationGuide: Start by defining who is authorised to access each physical zone and ensure your PACS (Lenel, Genetec) enforces this using badge-based access with no shared credentials. Configure the PACS to log all entry and exit events with timestamp and badge holder identity, and retain these logs for at least 12 months. Implement visitor management: require all visitors to sign in at reception using a digital visitor management system (Envoy, Proxyclick) that captures their name, host, purpose, and access agreement, and issue them a time-limited visitor badge that is visually distinct from staff badges. For highly restricted zones (server room, data centre), add a secondary authentication factor — PIN, biometric, or a second badge swipe — and review access logs monthly. Conduct quarterly access right reviews to remove access for leavers, role-changers, and anyone whose access is no longer justified. The done state is a PACS enforcing role-based zone access, a digital visitor management system in use, and quarterly access reviews with evidence. Auditors most commonly find failures where visitor logs are paper-based, incomplete, or visitor badges are collected but not invalidated after the visit.
exampleEvidence: PACS (Lenel, Genetec) access group configuration showing role-based access assignments and audit log retention policy set to 12 months
Envoy or Proxyclick visitor management system screenshot showing signed-in visitor log with name, host, purpose, and access agreement
Quarterly physical access review report showing access removed for leavers and role-changers, signed off by the facilities manager
Server room or data centre dual-authentication configuration (badge + PIN or biometric) screenshot showing secondary factor enforced
Visitor badge design photograph showing visual distinction from staff badges with time-limited validity clearly indicated
PACS entry/exit log extract for a restricted zone over a 30-day period showing all accesses attributed to named individuals with timestamps

title: Securing offices, rooms and facilities
description: Physical security for offices, rooms, and facilities must be designed and implemented.
guidance: Conduct a physical security walkthrough annually and address any findings within a risk-based remediation plan.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a room-by-room security assessment of all offices, meeting rooms, and facilities where sensitive discussions occur or confidential information is stored, documenting the current controls and gaps. For offices hosting sensitive information, ensure windows are frosted or fitted with privacy film where overlooking is possible, and that meeting rooms used for confidential discussions cannot be acoustically overheard from adjacent spaces. Configure your PACS to ensure office access is restricted to authorised staff, and that server rooms and comms rooms require secondary authentication. Lock filing cabinets and drawers containing physical documents classified as Confidential or Restricted, and conduct quarterly checks that locking is in place. Work with facilities management to ensure cleaners and maintenance staff have supervised or restricted-hours access to sensitive areas. The done state is a documented room security assessment with all identified gaps remediated, PACS enforcing office access, and physical security of document storage verified quarterly. Auditors most commonly find failures where offices are technically secured but visitors are left unescorted in areas adjacent to sensitive information — tailgating into open-plan offices is a persistent risk.
exampleEvidence: Room-by-room physical security assessment document showing controls evaluated, gaps identified, and remediation status for each room
PACS configuration showing office zone access restricted to authorised staff with visitor escort requirement enforced
Photographs of frosted window film, privacy screens, and locked filing cabinets installed in sensitive areas
Facilities management agreement extract showing cleaners and maintenance staff access restricted to approved hours with supervision requirement
Quarterly physical security check log confirming filing cabinets and sensitive document storage locked, with checker's name and date
Physical penetration test finding (or internal inspection report) confirming no unauthorised access achieved to restricted office areas

title: Physical security monitoring
description: Premises must be continuously monitored for unauthorised physical access.
guidance: Install CCTV covering all entry/exit points and server room access; retain footage for a minimum of 30 days.
implementationGuide: Start by defining the scope of your physical security monitoring: identify all locations requiring CCTV coverage — entrances, server rooms, data centre floors, car parks, and loading bays — and map camera positions to ensure no blind spots at critical entry points. Deploy a CCTV system with at minimum 1080p resolution, configure it to retain footage for at least 30 days (90 days for highly sensitive areas), and ensure footage is stored in a tamper-evident system. Integrate your CCTV and PACS with a physical security monitoring platform (Genetec Security Center, Milestone XProtect) and configure automated alerts for motion detection in restricted areas outside business hours or for door-forced events. Ensure monitoring is conducted in compliance with your jurisdiction's CCTV Code of Practice (ICO Code in the UK) — display appropriate signage and conduct a CCTV DPIA. Review camera coverage and system health monthly. The done state is a CCTV system covering all critical locations with 30+ day retention, integrated with PACS alerts, compliant with ICO Code, and with a documented review cadence. Auditors most commonly find failures where cameras exist but footage is overwritten too quickly to be useful for an investigation triggered 30+ days after an event.
exampleEvidence: CCTV camera coverage map showing all installed cameras, their positions, and coverage areas with no blind spots at critical entry points
Genetec Security Center or Milestone XProtect retention configuration showing 30-day minimum retention for standard areas and 90-day for restricted zones
CCTV DPIA completed and signed by the DPO confirming compliance with ICO CCTV Code of Practice and GDPR
Automated alert configuration in the PACS/CCTV integration showing motion-in-restricted-area and door-forced event notifications
Monthly CCTV system health check log confirming all cameras operational, footage accessible, and retention policy enforced
Physical security signage photograph showing CCTV notification signs displayed at facility entrances in compliance with ICO Code

title: Protecting against physical and environmental threats
description: Protection against physical and environmental threats such as natural disasters, malicious attack or accidents must be designed and applied.
guidance: Assess environmental threats (flood, fire, power) for each facility and implement mitigations per a documented risk assessment.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting an environmental threat assessment for each facility — identify relevant risks such as flooding, fire, earthquake, extreme temperature, electrical surges, and physical attack — and document the likelihood and impact of each. For server rooms and data centres, install environmental monitoring sensors (temperature, humidity, water leak, smoke) connected to a monitoring platform (Nlyte, Schneider EcoStruxure) that generates alerts to the on-call team via PagerDuty when thresholds are exceeded. Install physical protections appropriate to identified threats: fire suppression systems (FM-200 or VESDA), UPS systems for power continuity, raised flooring in flood-risk areas, and caged racks with tamper detection in colocation facilities. Conduct an annual review of environmental controls and test fire suppression, UPS, and generator systems on a documented schedule. The done state is a documented environmental threat assessment, monitoring sensors in all critical facilities with active alerting, and annual test evidence for all physical protection systems. Auditors most commonly find failures where environmental monitoring exists but alerts are sent to an unmanned inbox — no one sees the alert until the damage has already occurred.
exampleEvidence: Environmental threat assessment document for each facility showing identified threats, likelihood/impact ratings, and mitigating controls
Nlyte or Schneider EcoStruxure environmental monitoring dashboard showing active temperature, humidity, and water leak sensors with threshold alerts configured
PagerDuty or OpsGenie alert configuration showing environmental threshold alerts routed to the 24/7 on-call team
Fire suppression system annual test certificate (FM-200 or VESDA) showing successful test with date and engineer sign-off
UPS and generator test report showing load testing completed within the last 12 months with runtime and capacity verified
Colocation facility resilience certification (Uptime Institute Tier rating or equivalent) confirming environmental protection standards met

title: Working in secure areas
description: Security measures for working in secure areas must be designed and applied.
guidance: Post clear rules in secure areas (no photography, clean-desk policy) and brief all staff who regularly access them.
implementationGuide: Start by defining what constitutes a 'secure area' in your organisation — server rooms, data centre floors, network comms rooms, cryptographic key management areas, and any room containing Restricted data — and document the specific working rules for each. Publish a Secure Areas Policy in Confluence that prohibits: unescorted visitors, personal recording devices (phones with cameras), eating and drinking, and working alone in server rooms (implement a two-person rule for maintenance activities). Configure PACS to log all access and generate alerts for solo access to critical areas. Require staff working in secure areas to undergo specific security briefing before first access. Conduct unannounced spot checks quarterly to verify compliance with secure area rules and document findings. The done state is a published Secure Areas Policy, PACS access logging and alerting configured, staff briefed before first secure area access, and quarterly spot check records. Auditors most commonly find failures where the rules exist but are not enforced — spot checks during the audit itself (e.g., auditors walking into the server room unescorted) are a common test.
exampleEvidence: Secure Areas Policy in Confluence defining secure area types, prohibited activities, and entry requirements with named policy owner
PACS access log for the server room over the last 30 days showing all access events attributed to named individuals with timestamps
Secure area briefing completion record showing all staff with server room access completed the pre-access security briefing
PACS alert configuration showing solo-access-to-critical-area alerts sent to security manager outside approved maintenance windows
Quarterly spot check report for secure areas showing compliance observations, any rule violations found, and corrective actions
Visitor escort policy extract and PACS visitor badge configuration showing time-limited access with mandatory escort requirement for all visitors

title: Clear desk and clear screen
description: Clear desk rules for papers and removable storage media and clear screen rules for information processing facilities must be defined and enforced.
guidance: Enforce auto-screen lock after 5 minutes of inactivity and include clear-desk in quarterly office audits.
implementationGuide: Start by publishing a Clear Desk and Clear Screen policy in Confluence that defines: documents must be stored in locked drawers or cabinets when unattended, screens must lock within 5 minutes of inactivity (or immediately when leaving the workstation), whiteboards containing sensitive information must be erased before leaving the room, and printers must be checked immediately after printing. Enforce screen lock technically through Microsoft Intune or Jamf device configuration profiles — set auto-lock to 5 minutes maximum and require password/biometric to unlock. For printers in open-plan offices, deploy Follow-Me Printing (Papercut, Equitrac) requiring badge swipe at the printer to release jobs, preventing sensitive printouts from sitting uncollected. Conduct unannounced clean desk inspections quarterly — score offices against a checklist and report results to management. The done state is a published policy, screen lock technically enforced via MDM, Follow-Me Printing deployed, and quarterly clean desk audit reports. Auditors commonly find failures where screen lock is required by policy but MDM is not deployed, so users can simply change their own timeout settings.
exampleEvidence: Clear Desk and Clear Screen policy in Confluence with specific timeout requirements, document storage obligations, and printer rules
Microsoft Intune or Jamf device configuration profile showing 5-minute screen lock enforcement applied to all managed endpoints
Papercut or Equitrac Follow-Me Printing configuration showing badge-release printing enforced for all network printers
Quarterly clean desk inspection report showing office-by-office compliance scores with any violations noted and corrective actions
MDM compliance report from Intune or Jamf confirming 100% of managed devices have screen lock policy applied and compliant
Photograph evidence from a clean desk inspection showing a compliant workstation with cleared desk and locked screen

title: Equipment siting and protection
description: Equipment must be sited and protected to reduce the risks from physical and environmental threats, and from unauthorised access.
guidance: Locate servers in locked racks within dedicated data rooms; ensure adequate cooling and power supply redundancy.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a physical layout review of your server rooms and office spaces to identify equipment placement risks — equipment should not be visible from public areas, should not be positioned where it could be accidentally damaged by foot traffic, and critical servers should be in locked racks with individually keyed or PACS-controlled rack doors. For offices, position workstations so that screens cannot be viewed by passers-by or from public areas — use privacy screens on all monitors in reception areas, hot-desking zones, and open-plan areas. Protect equipment from environmental hazards: ensure servers are not positioned below water pipes, near heat sources, or in areas susceptible to magnetic fields. Document approved equipment siting requirements in a facilities standard and include physical security review in the procurement process for new IT equipment. The done state is a documented equipment siting standard, all server equipment in locked racks with PACS-controlled access, privacy screens on public-facing workstations, and an annual siting review completed. Auditors most commonly find failures where network equipment (switches, patch panels) is installed in unlocked comms cupboards accessible to all staff.
exampleEvidence: Equipment siting standard document specifying placement requirements for servers, network equipment, and workstations by area type
Server room rack layout diagram showing all equipment in PACS-controlled locked racks with rack door access log configuration
Privacy screen installation photograph for workstations in reception and open-plan areas
Annual physical layout review report confirming equipment siting compliance with no critical findings, or findings and remediation plan
Network equipment audit showing all switches and patch panels installed in locked comms rooms with PACS access control
New IT equipment procurement checklist showing physical siting assessment as a mandatory step before installation sign-off

title: Security of assets off-premises
description: Off-site assets must be protected, taking into account the different risks of working outside the organisation's premises.
guidance: Encrypt all mobile devices and require remote-wipe capability; document the procedure for reporting lost or stolen devices.
implementationGuide: Start by creating an off-premises asset register that lists all corporate assets taken out of controlled facilities — laptops, mobile phones, portable storage, and any equipment taken home or to client sites. Configure your MDM (Microsoft Intune, Jamf) to enforce full-disk encryption (BitLocker or FileVault) on all portable devices, require a PIN/password to unlock, and enable remote wipe capability. Define a policy that prohibits storing Restricted data on portable devices unless encrypted and authorised — enforce this technically with Microsoft Purview DLP policies that prevent downloading Restricted-classified content to unmanaged or non-compliant devices. Require staff to report lost or stolen devices immediately through your incident reporting process so that remote wipe can be initiated within 1 hour of notification. Conduct a quarterly audit reconciling the off-premises asset register against MDM device inventory. The done state is an off-premises asset register reconciled quarterly, full-disk encryption enforced on 100% of portable devices, and a remote wipe capability tested annually. Auditors most commonly find failures where personal laptops used for remote work are not enrolled in MDM and have no encryption enforcement.
exampleEvidence: Off-premises asset register showing all corporate assets taken off-site with serial number, assigned user, and last check-in date
Microsoft Intune or Jamf compliance report showing 100% of portable devices with BitLocker or FileVault full-disk encryption enforced
Microsoft Purview DLP policy configuration showing restriction on downloading Restricted-classified content to non-compliant or unmanaged devices
Remote wipe capability test record showing successful wipe initiated and confirmed for a test device within 1-hour SLA
Incident report for a lost or stolen device showing remote wipe initiated within 1 hour of staff notification, with timeline documented
Quarterly asset register reconciliation report showing off-premises assets matched against MDM inventory with any discrepancies investigated

title: Storage media
description: Storage media must be managed through its lifecycle of acquisition, use, transportation, and disposal in accordance with the classification scheme.
guidance: Maintain a media register, use encrypted media for Confidential+ data, and apply NIST 800-88 sanitisation before disposal.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a storage media management policy covering the full lifecycle: procurement, use, classification labelling, transfer, archival, and secure disposal. Maintain an inventory of all removable storage media (USB drives, external HDDs, backup tapes, optical media) in your asset management system, with each item assigned an owner, classification level, and location. For USB drives, enforce your endpoint DLP policy (Microsoft Intune or Symantec Endpoint Protection) to block unapproved USB devices — use Microsoft Intune's removable storage access control to allow only approved, encrypted corporate USB drives. For backup media, store tapes or drives in a fireproof, environmentally controlled safe on-site and a separate secure facility off-site. When media reaches end of life, use certified data destruction (NIST 800-88 compliant) — either degauss, secure overwrite using Blancco, or physical shredding — and obtain a destruction certificate. The done state is a media inventory, USB block policy enforced via MDM, encrypted corporate USB drives for approved transfers, and certified destruction certificates on file. Auditors most commonly find failures where USB access is not technically blocked — policy alone without technical enforcement is not sufficient.
exampleEvidence: Storage media inventory in asset management system showing all removable media with owner, classification, location, and status
Microsoft Intune removable storage access control policy screenshot showing unapproved USB devices blocked on all managed endpoints
Approved encrypted corporate USB drive specification and procurement record showing only encrypted drives issued to staff requiring portable storage
Backup media off-site storage agreement with a secure facility showing environmental and access control requirements
Blancco or certified shredder destruction certificate for decommissioned storage media showing NIST 800-88 compliant destruction method
Monthly USB block alert log from Intune showing detected and blocked attempts to connect unapproved USB devices

title: Supporting utilities
description: Information processing facilities must be protected from power failures and other disruptions caused by failures in supporting utilities.
guidance: Install UPS and generator backup for critical systems and test them under load at least annually.
implementationGuide: Start by auditing all supporting utilities for your critical IT facilities — power, cooling, water (for cooling), telecommunications, and fire suppression — and document each utility, its capacity, redundancy level, and single points of failure. For power, install UPS systems sized for at least 15 minutes of runtime for the full load, and a diesel generator for extended outages — test both monthly (UPS self-test) and annually (full generator load test). For cooling, ensure CRAC/CRAH units are N+1 redundant and configure temperature monitoring in your DCIM tool (Nlyte, Schneider EcoStruxure) with alerts if temperature exceeds 27°C. Subscribe to utility supplier outage notification services and include utility failure scenarios in your BCP. The done state is a documented utility audit, UPS and generator tested on schedule with test certificates, redundant cooling with monitoring, and utility failure included in BCP. Auditors most commonly find failures when UPS units have never been load-tested and their actual runtime is unknown — a UPS that shows 'healthy' on self-test may still fail under full load.
exampleEvidence: Supporting utilities audit document showing power, cooling, comms, and fire suppression specifications, redundancy levels, and single points of failure
UPS annual load test certificate showing runtime achieved under full load, conducted within the last 12 months
Diesel generator annual load test report showing successful start, run time at rated load, and fuel consumption
Nlyte or EcoStruxure DCIM temperature monitoring dashboard showing sensor locations, threshold alerts, and historical temperature data
CRAC/CRAH unit N+1 redundancy configuration diagram and monthly maintenance log confirming all units operational
BCP section showing utility failure scenario with recovery procedures and RTO targets for IT systems dependent on affected utilities

title: Cabling security
description: Cables carrying power or data or supporting information services must be protected from interception, interference, or damage.
guidance: Label all cables, use conduit in accessible areas, and document the network cabling layout.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a cabling audit of all your facilities — document the routing of power cables, structured data cabling, and fibre runs, and ensure they are separated to prevent electromagnetic interference between power and data cables. For server rooms and data centres, use cable management trays, colour-coding standards, and cable labels at both ends of every run — document the labelling scheme in a cabling standard stored in Confluence. Protect cables in public and accessible areas by routing them through conduit, under raised flooring, or within cable management systems that are not easily accessible. Inspect cabling in critical areas quarterly and after any maintenance work. For external comms lines entering the facility, ensure provider SLAs include physical protection commitments, and consider diverse routing for critical connectivity. The done state is a documented cabling plan with labelling standards, quarterly inspection records, and physical protection of cables in all accessible areas. Auditors most commonly find failures where cabling in comms rooms is a disorganised 'spaghetti' mess — it indicates poor change management and makes fault diagnosis and unauthorised tapping much harder to detect.
exampleEvidence: Cabling plan document showing physical cable routing for power and data, with separation compliance and labelling scheme defined
Server room cabling photographs showing organised, labelled cable runs in management trays with colour-coding scheme applied
Quarterly cabling inspection report for critical areas showing any issues found, corrective actions, and sign-off by network engineer
Cable labelling standard document defining label format, colour coding, and documentation requirements for both ends of each run
External comms provider SLA extract showing physical protection and diverse routing commitments for critical connectivity
Maintenance work completion report showing cabling inspection conducted after a recent infrastructure change, confirming no damage or unauthorised modifications

title: Equipment maintenance
description: Equipment must be maintained correctly to ensure availability and integrity of information.
guidance: Follow manufacturer maintenance schedules and retain service records for all critical hardware.
implementationGuide: Start by creating an equipment maintenance schedule for all in-scope hardware — servers, network switches, UPS systems, CRAC/CRAH units, fire suppression, CCTV, and PACS — using your CMDB (ServiceNow) or a dedicated maintenance management tool. For manufacturer-supported equipment, follow the OEM maintenance intervals and ensure valid support contracts are in place before warranties expire. For critical systems, require that only authorised service engineers (vetted and identified) perform maintenance, and escort them in secure areas — log all maintenance access in the PACS and the maintenance record. Implement a pre- and post-maintenance check process to verify equipment state before and after any maintenance activity. Keep all maintenance records in your CMDB with engineer name, activity performed, outcome, and next scheduled date. The done state is a maintenance schedule with 100% of critical equipment on a supported maintenance programme, maintenance records for all completed activities, and authorized-engineer-only access evidenced in PACS logs. Auditors most commonly find failures where equipment is past its end of support date with no valid maintenance contract — particularly common with legacy network hardware.
exampleEvidence: Equipment maintenance schedule in ServiceNow CMDB showing all critical hardware with maintenance intervals, next due date, and contract status
OEM maintenance contract or extended support agreement for critical servers and network equipment showing coverage dates and response SLAs
PACS access log showing authorised maintenance engineer badge access to the server room with corresponding maintenance record in CMDB
Pre- and post-maintenance check form completed for a recent maintenance activity showing equipment state verified before and after work
Completed maintenance work order from a recent activity showing engineer name, company, work performed, outcome, and sign-off
End-of-support register showing any hardware approaching end of support with replacement or extended support plan documented

title: Secure disposal or re-use of equipment
description: Items of equipment containing storage media must be verified to ensure that any sensitive data and licensed software has been removed or securely overwritten prior to disposal or re-use.
guidance: Use certified data destruction services with written certificates of destruction; document the disposal process.
implementationGuide: Start by creating a secure disposal and re-use procedure for all types of equipment — laptops, servers, mobile phones, storage media, printers, and networking equipment — covering both internal re-use and disposal to third parties. Before any equipment leaves your control, require that all data is securely erased using a NIST 800-88 compliant method: for HDDs, use Blancco or DBAN overwrite; for SSDs and NVMe drives, use cryptographic erasure (if self-encrypting) or physical destruction; for mobile devices, perform factory reset with activation lock cleared. Engage a certified IT asset disposal (ITAD) vendor (certified to e-Stewards or R2 standards) for equipment you cannot process in-house, and obtain a data destruction certificate and asset serialisation report for every disposal. Maintain a decommissioning register tracking each asset from CMDB decommission request through data erasure to physical disposal with chain of custody. The done state is a documented disposal procedure, ITAD vendor with certification on file, destruction certificates for all disposals in the last 12 months, and a decommissioning register. Auditors most commonly find failures where employees take decommissioned laptops home for 'personal use' without any data erasure — a category of data breach often missed by organisations.
exampleEvidence: Secure disposal and re-use procedure document covering all equipment types with NIST 800-88 compliant erasure method specified for each
Blancco erasure report for a recently decommissioned laptop showing drive serial number, erasure method, result, and date
ITAD vendor certification (e-Stewards or R2 standard) and destruction certificate for equipment disposed of in the last 12 months
Decommissioning register showing all assets from CMDB decommission request through data erasure to final disposal with chain of custody
Mobile device factory reset and activation lock clearance procedure with completion evidence for a recently decommissioned device
CMDB decommission workflow screenshot showing mandatory data erasure verification step before asset status updated to 'disposed'

title: User endpoint devices
description: Information stored on, processed by, or accessible via user endpoint devices must be protected.
guidance: Deploy MDM/UEM on all endpoints enforcing encryption, EDR, and OS patch currency within 14 days of release.
implementationGuide: Start by maintaining a comprehensive inventory of all user endpoint devices (laptops, workstations, mobile devices) using Microsoft Intune or Jamf Pro, ensuring every asset is enrolled before network access is granted. Configure device compliance policies that enforce disk encryption (BitLocker for Windows, FileVault for macOS), screen lock timeouts under five minutes, and current OS patch levels as prerequisites for Conditional Access. Deploy CrowdStrike Falcon or Microsoft Defender for Endpoint on every managed device to provide real-time EDR telemetry and enforce baseline security posture. Establish a device configuration baseline using CIS Benchmarks and validate compliance continuously through Intune compliance reports or Jamf compliance dashboards — non-compliant devices must be automatically quarantined from corporate resources. Implement a Mobile Device Management (MDM) policy that requires device registration before email or SaaS application access is provisioned, and use Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access to enforce this gate. The observable done state is a real-time dashboard showing 100% of known endpoints enrolled, compliant, and reporting telemetry with zero unmanaged devices accessing corporate data. A common audit failure mode is discovering shadow IT devices — personally owned or unregistered machines — accessing corporate resources via saved credentials or unprotected Wi-Fi, which auditors flag immediately when reviewing Conditional Access sign-in logs.
exampleEvidence: Intune or Jamf device compliance report showing 100% enrollment rate with compliance status per device (exported within 30 days of audit)
CrowdStrike Falcon or Defender for Endpoint sensor coverage report confirming EDR agent installed on all managed endpoints
BitLocker/FileVault encryption status report for all enrolled devices, signed off by IT Security Lead
Conditional Access policy screenshot showing 'Require compliant device' enforced for all cloud app access
Asset register extract listing all authorised endpoint devices with assigned owner, department, and last-seen date
Incident ticket or exception log showing how non-compliant devices are quarantined and remediated within defined SLA

title: Privileged access rights
description: The allocation and use of privileged access rights must be restricted and managed.
guidance: Implement PAM tooling, enforce just-in-time access, and log all privileged sessions with quarterly review.
implementationGuide: Start by enumerating all privileged accounts — local admin, domain admin, service accounts, and cloud IAM roles — using a discovery scan in CyberArk Privileged Access Manager or BeyondTrust Password Safe, and document each account in a privileged account register with business justification. Implement just-in-time (JIT) privileged access so that elevated rights are granted on demand for a time-boxed session (typically 1-4 hours) and automatically revoked, using CyberArk or Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM). Enforce multi-factor authentication for all privileged sessions and record every session with full keystroke and video logging so that post-incident forensics are possible. Rotate credentials for all service and shared accounts on a defined schedule (90 days or less) and store them exclusively in a secrets manager such as HashiCorp Vault or CyberArk, never in scripts or configuration files. Conduct quarterly privileged access reviews where each account's business owner certifies continued need; revoke any account without a certified owner immediately. The observable done state is a PAM tool report showing zero standing privileged accounts, all sessions recorded, and a completed quarterly access review with documented approvals. A common audit failure mode is discovering long-lived service accounts with never-expiring passwords stored in plaintext configuration files, which auditors reliably uncover by requesting a secrets scan or reviewing CI/CD pipeline configuration.
exampleEvidence: CyberArk or BeyondTrust privileged account register listing all accounts with owner, justification, and last-review date
Microsoft Entra PIM or CyberArk JIT access policy screenshot showing time-boxed elevation with MFA enforcement
Privileged session recording sample (metadata summary) from CyberArk Privileged Session Manager covering the audit period
HashiCorp Vault or CyberArk credential rotation log showing automated password rotation within the policy-defined schedule
Quarterly privileged access recertification sign-off document with owner signatures and list of revoked accounts

title: Information access restriction
description: Access to information and other associated assets must be restricted in accordance with the established topic-specific policy on access control.
guidance: Apply need-to-know access to all datastores holding Confidential+ data and review permissions quarterly.
implementationGuide: Start by documenting a formal access control policy that defines information classification levels and maps each level to permitted user roles, following the principle of least privilege. Implement role-based access control (RBAC) in Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) or Okta, creating groups that mirror job functions rather than granting permissions directly to individuals, and enforce Conditional Access policies that restrict sensitive data access to compliant, managed devices. Use Microsoft Purview Information Protection or Varonis Data Security Platform to classify sensitive data stores and apply access controls automatically based on classification labels. Conduct semi-annual access reviews using Saviynt or SailPoint IdentityNow to recertify every user's access rights; any access not positively affirmed by a business owner within the review window must be automatically revoked. Integrate data access controls with your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) to alert on anomalous access patterns such as bulk downloads, access outside business hours, or access from unexpected geographies. The observable done state is a complete role-to-permission matrix approved by data owners, zero accounts with access beyond their documented role, and a completed semi-annual review with no overdue certifications. A common audit failure mode is role accumulation — users who changed roles but retained prior access rights — which auditors find by cross-referencing HR transfer records against access review reports.
exampleEvidence: RBAC role matrix document mapping job titles to system permissions, approved and signed by CISO or IT Director
Microsoft Entra ID or Okta group membership report showing access granted exclusively via role-defined groups
Microsoft Purview or Varonis data classification and access control policy configuration screenshot for sensitive repositories
Semi-annual access recertification completion report from SailPoint or Saviynt, including list of revoked access entitlements
Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel alert rule configured for anomalous data access patterns with sample triggered alert
HR joiner-mover-leaver process document showing access provisioning and de-provisioning tied to HR system events

title: Access to source code
description: Read and write access to source code, development tools, and software libraries must be appropriately managed.
guidance: Restrict production repository write access to named developers and enforce code review before merge.
implementationGuide: Start by inventorying all repositories containing production source code — GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or Azure DevOps — and classify them by sensitivity, applying branch protection rules so that direct commits to main/master are blocked and all changes require a pull request with at least one independent reviewer. Restrict repository access using the principle of least privilege: developers get read/write to their own project repositories only, and access to repositories outside their team requires explicit approval tracked via an access request ticket in Jira or ServiceNow. Enable secret scanning in GitHub Advanced Security or GitLab Ultimate to prevent credentials, API keys, and certificates from being committed, and configure pre-commit hooks using tools like git-secrets or detect-secrets to catch secrets before they reach the remote. Audit repository access quarterly using your source control platform's audit log or a CASB such as Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps, and immediately revoke access for any departed employee or contractor identified via HR offboarding triggers. Implement code signing requirements for release builds so that only reviewed and approved code reaches production, and store code-signing certificates in HashiCorp Vault or AWS KMS. The observable done state is a branch protection policy report showing all production repositories protected, a completed quarterly access review, and zero unresolved secret-scanning alerts. A common audit failure mode is orphaned service account tokens with broad repository access created for CI/CD pipelines but never rotated or scoped, which auditors find by requesting a machine-user access report.
exampleEvidence: GitHub or GitLab branch protection rules screenshot showing required PR reviews and direct-push restrictions on all production repositories
Repository access control report listing users and their permission levels, generated from GitHub Audit Log or GitLab Admin panel
GitHub Advanced Security or GitLab secret scanning alert history showing zero open high-severity findings
Quarterly source code access recertification record with approvals and list of revoked accounts
CI/CD service account access policy document limiting tokens to repository-scoped read permissions with rotation schedule
Code-signing certificate management procedure referencing HashiCorp Vault or AWS KMS as the authoritative secret store

title: Secure authentication
description: Secure authentication technologies and procedures must be implemented based on information access restrictions and the topic-specific policy on access control.
guidance: Enforce MFA for all remote access, admin consoles, and cloud management portals.
implementationGuide: Start by enforcing multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all user accounts across every system boundary — corporate SSO (Okta, Microsoft Entra ID), VPN, cloud consoles, and privileged jump servers — with phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys or Microsoft Authenticator number matching) preferred over SMS OTP. Define and enforce a password policy in your identity provider that mandates a minimum length of 16 characters for human accounts, bans commonly breached passwords (checked against HaveIBeenPwned or Entra ID Password Protection), and prohibits password reuse for the last 10 cycles. Store all service account and application credentials in HashiCorp Vault or CyberArk, with automated rotation on a 90-day schedule and dynamic secrets for database credentials where possible. Eliminate shared credentials entirely by provisioning individual accounts for every user and auditing any shared mailbox or shared admin account to confirm MFA is enforced at the application level. Monitor authentication events in Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel with rules alerting on password spray patterns, impossible travel, and repeated MFA failures to detect credential-based attacks in real time. The observable done state is an MFA coverage report showing 100% of accounts protected, a password policy configuration export, and a vault secrets rotation audit log with no overdue rotations. A common audit failure mode is VPN or legacy application authentication falling back to username and password only because MFA was not enforced at the application layer, which auditors discover by inspecting authentication logs for sessions lacking an MFA claim.
exampleEvidence: Okta or Microsoft Entra ID MFA enforcement policy screenshot showing all users required to use phishing-resistant MFA
Password policy configuration export from Active Directory or Entra ID confirming minimum length, complexity, and history requirements
HashiCorp Vault or CyberArk credential rotation log showing all service account passwords rotated within the 90-day window
Splunk or Sentinel alert rule for password spray detection with sample alert fired during the audit period
MFA coverage report showing percentage of accounts enrolled, broken down by authentication method type
Offboarding checklist extract showing credential revocation steps completed within one business day of employee departure

title: Capacity management
description: The use of resources must be monitored and adjusted, and projections made of future capacity requirements.
guidance: Set monitoring thresholds at 80% for CPU, memory, and storage, with automated alerting and a quarterly capacity review.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a cryptography policy that specifies approved algorithms (AES-256 for symmetric, RSA-4096 or ECDSA P-384 for asymmetric, SHA-256 or higher for hashing) and prohibited algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4), and distribute it to all development and infrastructure teams. Centralise all cryptographic key management in HashiCorp Vault, AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or a hardware security module (HSM), ensuring no key material ever exists in plaintext outside these systems. Enforce key lifecycle management: generate keys in the HSM or KMS, set expiry dates aligned to the cryptography policy (typically 1-2 years for data encryption keys, shorter for signing keys), automate rotation, and log every key operation (creation, rotation, use, deletion) for audit trail. Integrate key management with your certificate lifecycle tool — Venafi or DigiCert CertCentral — to automate TLS certificate renewal and alert on certificates expiring within 60 days, eliminating manual renewal failures. Conduct an annual cryptography review to assess algorithm strengths against current threat intelligence (including post-quantum readiness planning) and update the approved algorithm list accordingly. The observable done state is a KMS key inventory showing all active keys with defined expiry, a completed annual cryptography review document, and zero expired certificates or deprecated algorithms in production. A common audit failure mode is hardcoded encryption keys found in source code or configuration files, which auditors detect by requesting a secrets scan output or reviewing a sample of application configuration files.
exampleEvidence: Cryptography policy document listing approved and prohibited algorithms with version history and CISO approval signature
AWS KMS or HashiCorp Vault key inventory report showing all keys with creation date, expiry date, and rotation schedule
Venafi or DigiCert CertCentral certificate inventory showing zero expired or soon-to-expire (under 30 days) TLS certificates
HSM or KMS audit log extract showing key creation, rotation, and access events for the audit period
Annual cryptography review meeting minutes and updated algorithm register signed by the Security Architecture team
GitHub Advanced Security or Semgrep scan results confirming zero hardcoded cryptographic keys in application repositories

title: Protection against malware
description: Protection against malware must be implemented and supported by appropriate user awareness.
guidance: Deploy EDR/AV on all endpoints and servers with real-time protection enabled and signatures updated at least daily.
implementationGuide: Start by publishing a clear desk and clear screen policy that defines what constitutes a violation (physical documents, removable media, unlocked screens) and the consequences, then communicate it to all staff through awareness training tracked in your LMS. Configure Group Policy or Jamf to enforce an automatic screen lock after no more than five minutes of inactivity across all managed endpoints, and verify compliance through Intune or Jamf compliance reports. Conduct monthly physical security walk-throughs using a standardised checklist to identify violations such as unattended sensitive documents, unlocked filing cabinets, or whiteboards containing confidential data, and record findings in your GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC). Implement secure print-release functionality on all networked printers (Papercut or equivalent) so that print jobs are held in a queue until the user authenticates at the device, preventing sensitive documents from sitting uncollected. Track exceptions and repeat offenders through the GRC platform and escalate persistent violations to line management with documented evidence. The observable done state is a compliance report showing all managed devices enforce screen lock within the policy timeout, completed monthly walk-through records with zero critical open findings, and 100% staff completion of clear desk awareness training. A common audit failure mode is screen lock policy applied to domain-joined machines but not to personal devices or Mac endpoints managed separately, leaving gaps auditors find by spot-testing devices in the office.
exampleEvidence: Intune or Jamf compliance report confirming screen lock policy (≤5 minutes inactivity) applied to 100% of managed endpoints
Monthly physical security walk-through checklist and findings log for the last three months, signed by the Facilities Security Officer
PaperCut or equivalent secure print configuration screenshot showing authenticated print-release enabled on all networked printers
LMS training completion report showing 100% of staff completed clear desk and screen lock awareness training in the current year
Clear desk and screen policy document with version history, approval signature, and staff acknowledgement log
GRC platform exception log showing any violations raised, remediation actions, and closure dates

title: Management of technical vulnerabilities
description: Information about technical vulnerabilities of information systems in use must be obtained, the organisation's exposure evaluated, and appropriate measures taken.
guidance: Run authenticated vulnerability scans monthly; patch Critical CVEs within 48 hours and High within 7 days.
implementationGuide: Start by deploying Tenable.io, Qualys VMDR, or Rapid7 InsightVM as your vulnerability management platform, configuring authenticated scans for all in-scope assets (servers, endpoints, network devices, containers) on a weekly schedule for internal systems and daily for internet-facing assets. Define a vulnerability remediation SLA in your vulnerability management policy: critical (CVSS 9.0+) within 15 days, high (7.0-8.9) within 30 days, medium (4.0-6.9) within 90 days, and track every finding against these SLAs in your ticketing system (Jira or ServiceNow). Integrate vulnerability scan results with your CMDB and SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) so that asset risk scores inform prioritisation and new critical findings trigger automatic alert escalation to the security team. For container environments, integrate Snyk Container or Prisma Cloud into the CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) to block deployments that introduce new critical or high vulnerabilities before they reach production. Conduct penetration tests at least annually (and after significant infrastructure changes) using a qualified external firm, and track all findings through to closure in the GRC platform. The observable done state is a vulnerability dashboard showing zero overdue critical and high findings, all assets scanned within the defined frequency, and a completed annual penetration test report with remediation evidence. A common audit failure mode is vulnerability scan coverage gaps — assets added to production without being enrolled in the scanner — which auditors expose by comparing the CMDB or cloud inventory against the scanner asset list.
exampleEvidence: Tenable.io or Qualys VMDR scan configuration screenshot showing authenticated weekly scans for all in-scope assets
Vulnerability remediation SLA tracking report from Jira or ServiceNow showing zero overdue critical/high findings
Most recent penetration test executive report with scope, findings, and remediation closure evidence
Snyk or Prisma Cloud CI/CD pipeline scan policy blocking deployments with critical vulnerabilities, with pipeline run example
Vulnerability management policy document defining SLA tiers, roles, and escalation procedures, signed by CISO
CMDB vs vulnerability scanner asset reconciliation report confirming 100% scan coverage with no unscanned assets

title: Configuration management
description: Configurations, including security configurations, of hardware, software, services, and networks must be established, documented, implemented, monitored, and reviewed.
guidance: Define hardened configuration baselines (e.g. CIS Benchmarks) for all OS types and enforce via configuration management tooling.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing a configuration management policy that mandates all infrastructure be provisioned using infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Ansible, or AWS CloudFormation) and that approved security baselines (CIS Benchmarks Level 1 or 2) are codified as configuration templates stored in version-controlled repositories. Deploy an enterprise configuration management tool — Chef InSpec, Ansible Automation Platform, or Puppet — to continuously assess and enforce configuration compliance across all servers, cloud instances, and network devices, generating automated remediation for drift detected against the baseline. Integrate configuration scanning into your CI/CD pipeline using tools such as Checkov or tfsec to catch insecure configurations before infrastructure is deployed, with pipeline policies blocking non-compliant templates. Use AWS Config, Azure Policy, or Google Cloud Security Command Center to enforce guardrails on cloud resource configurations (e.g., no public S3 buckets, encryption at rest mandatory, logging enabled) and alert on any policy violation in near real time. Maintain a configuration item (CI) register in your CMDB (ServiceNow) with approved baseline versions for each asset class, and require a change request for any deviation from baseline. The observable done state is a configuration compliance dashboard showing 95%+ of assets compliant with the approved baseline, zero unreviewed configuration drift events, and all infrastructure provisioned via approved IaC pipelines.
