A common audit failure mode is manual configuration changes made directly on production servers bypassing the IaC pipeline, which auditors find by comparing running configurations against the IaC repository state.
exampleEvidence: Terraform or Ansible repository showing infrastructure-as-code templates with CIS Benchmark baseline configuration enforced
Chef InSpec or Ansible compliance scan report showing per-asset compliance percentage against defined security baseline
Checkov or tfsec CI/CD pipeline scan results blocking non-compliant infrastructure templates from deployment
AWS Config or Azure Policy compliance dashboard screenshot showing enforced guardrails and zero critical non-compliant resources
Configuration management policy document defining baseline standards, change control requirements, and deviation approval process
ServiceNow CMDB configuration item register extract showing approved baseline version and last compliance check date per asset

title: Information deletion
description: Information stored in information systems, devices, or in any other storage media must be deleted when no longer required.
guidance: Automate data deletion workflows based on the records retention schedule and log all deletions for audit.
implementationGuide: Start by classifying all data stores by sensitivity and defining retention and disposal requirements in a data retention schedule approved by Legal, Compliance, and the CISO, covering structured databases, file shares, cloud storage, backups, and removable media. Implement automated data lifecycle policies in Microsoft Purview (retention labels and policies), AWS S3 Lifecycle, or Azure Blob Lifecycle Management to enforce retention periods and trigger secure deletion when data reaches its end-of-life date. For physical media disposal, contract with a certified destruction vendor (NAID AAA certified) and obtain a Certificate of Destruction for every media disposal event; maintain a media disposal register tracking asset serial numbers, disposal dates, and destruction certificates. Configure Data Loss Prevention policies in Microsoft Purview or Symantec DLP to detect and block transmission of data types subject to retention or disposal restrictions, such as regulated personal data, to unauthorised external destinations. Conduct an annual data inventory and mapping exercise to identify new data stores, validate existing retention schedules, and confirm automated disposal policies are correctly applied. The observable done state is an automated retention policy applied to all classified data stores, a complete media disposal register for the audit period, and a current data inventory map approved by data owners. A common audit failure mode is discovering stale data (e.g., 10-year-old customer records) in cloud storage with no retention policy applied because the bucket was created before lifecycle management was mandated.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Purview or AWS S3 Lifecycle retention policy configuration showing automated deletion rules per data classification
Data retention schedule document approved by Legal, Compliance, and CISO, with retention periods per data category
NAID-certified destruction vendor Certificate of Destruction for all physical media disposed of during the audit period
Media disposal register listing asset serial numbers, disposal dates, and references to destruction certificates
Microsoft Purview DLP policy configuration blocking exfiltration of data subject to retention controls to unauthorised destinations
Annual data inventory and mapping exercise report confirming all data stores identified, classified, and covered by retention policy

title: Data masking
description: Data masking must be used in accordance with the organisation's topic-specific policy on access control and other related topic-specific policies, and business requirements.
guidance: Mask PII and PAN in non-production environments and in user-facing outputs where full data is not required.
implementationGuide: Start by documenting a data masking and anonymisation standard that defines which data elements require masking (PII, payment card data, health information) in non-production environments, the approved masking techniques (tokenisation, pseudonymisation, format-preserving encryption), and the tools authorised for each use case. Implement dynamic data masking in production databases using Microsoft SQL Server Dynamic Data Masking, Oracle Data Masking and Subsetting, or Delphix to restrict sensitive field visibility for non-privileged query users without altering stored data. For non-production environment data provisioning, use Delphix or IBM InfoSphere Optim to generate masked copies of production data sets, and automate this process within your CI/CD pipeline so that test environments never contain real PII. Integrate masking controls with your Microsoft Purview or Varonis data governance platform to ensure masked data stores are catalogued and access controls are enforced consistently with the classification policy. Audit test and development environments quarterly to confirm no unmasked production data is present, using a data discovery scan with Spirion or BigID to detect PII patterns. The observable done state is a data masking policy applied to all non-production copies of sensitive data, a quarterly audit report confirming zero unmasked PII in test environments, and dynamic data masking rules active in production for all restricted fields. A common audit failure mode is database dumps or CSV exports of production data loaded directly into a developer's local environment, bypassing masking controls entirely, which auditors uncover by interviewing developers or reviewing file transfer logs.
exampleEvidence: Data masking and anonymisation standard document listing which fields require masking, approved techniques, and authorised tools
Delphix or IBM InfoSphere Optim masking job configuration and execution log showing masked data sets provisioned to non-production environments
SQL Server Dynamic Data Masking or Oracle masking rule configuration screenshot for production sensitive fields
Quarterly non-production environment PII scan report from Spirion or BigID confirming zero unmasked sensitive data found
Microsoft Purview data catalogue extract showing masked data stores classified and access-controlled per policy
CI/CD pipeline configuration showing automated masked data provisioning step before non-production environment deployment

title: Data leakage prevention
description: Data leakage prevention measures must be applied to systems, networks, and other devices that process, store, or transmit sensitive information.
guidance: Deploy DLP policies covering email, cloud uploads, and removable media for all Confidential+ data classifications.
implementationGuide: Start by deploying a Data Loss Prevention solution — Microsoft Purview DLP, Forcepoint DLP, or Symantec DLP — across email, endpoint, cloud storage (OneDrive, SharePoint, Google Drive), and web proxy egress points, and configure policies aligned to your data classification scheme to detect and block unauthorised exfiltration of sensitive data. Define DLP policy rules for high-priority data types: credit card numbers (PCI), national ID numbers, source code patterns, and documents labelled 'Confidential' or 'Restricted', with actions ranging from user coaching (low-risk) to automatic block and alert (high-risk). Integrate DLP alerting with your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) and configure a Security Operations playbook for DLP incidents, including evidence preservation, user notification, and manager escalation within defined SLAs. Extend coverage to cloud applications using a CASB — Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps or Netskope — to enforce DLP policies on SaaS apps such as Salesforce, Slack, and Box that may not be covered by the endpoint DLP agent. Conduct a monthly DLP policy effectiveness review examining false positive rates, blocked events, and any policy bypasses, and tune policies to maintain accuracy above 90%. The observable done state is a DLP dashboard showing policy coverage across all egress channels, a monthly effectiveness report, and zero unreviewed high-severity DLP incidents in the queue. A common audit failure mode is DLP policies configured in 'audit only' mode for months after deployment because production blocking was never formally approved, leaving exfiltration risk unmitigated while creating a false sense of control.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Purview or Forcepoint DLP policy configuration showing data classification-aligned rules across email, endpoint, and cloud egress
DLP incident log for the audit period showing event counts, severities, actions taken (blocked vs. alerted), and resolution status
Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps or Netskope CASB policy screenshot showing DLP enforcement on SaaS applications
Splunk or Sentinel DLP incident response playbook and sample closed incident ticket with evidence preservation steps
Monthly DLP policy effectiveness review report showing false positive rate, blocked event count, and policy tuning actions
Data classification labelling policy showing sensitivity labels applied in Microsoft Purview Information Protection for Confidential data

title: Information backup
description: Backup copies of information, software, and systems must be maintained and regularly tested in accordance with the agreed topic-specific policy on backup.
guidance: Follow the 3-2-1 backup rule and test restores quarterly; document target RTO/RPO per system.
implementationGuide: Start by conducting a business impact analysis (BIA) to identify critical information systems and define recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for each, documenting the results in a formal business continuity and disaster recovery plan reviewed and approved by senior management. Implement automated, encrypted backups for all critical systems using Veeam Backup & Replication, AWS Backup, or Azure Backup, with backup frequency aligned to RPO (e.g., hourly incremental, daily full for RTO < 4 hours), and store copies in geographically separated locations or immutable cloud storage to protect against ransomware. Configure monitoring of backup job success in your SIEM (Splunk) or backup platform's alerting to ensure every backup is verified within 24 hours of completion, and page on-call engineers for any failed backup job. Conduct full restoration tests at least quarterly, validating that restored data is consistent, applications function correctly post-restoration, and RTO targets are achievable; document test results including time-to-restore metrics. Maintain an offsite or cloud-replicated copy of backup data subject to the same access controls as production, using AWS S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage to enforce WORM (write once, read many) retention. The observable done state is a backup coverage report confirming 100% of BIA-critical systems are backed up within RPO, a completed quarterly restoration test report, and an immutable storage configuration audit. A common audit failure mode is backups that have never been restoration-tested — auditors regularly find that backup jobs succeed but restores fail due to configuration drift, catalogue corruption, or expired encryption keys.
exampleEvidence: Veeam, AWS Backup, or Azure Backup job success report for the audit period showing 100% of critical systems backed up within RPO
Business impact analysis document listing critical systems with approved RTO and RPO targets, signed by executive sponsor
Quarterly backup restoration test report including restored system, validation steps performed, time-to-restore, and sign-off
AWS S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage configuration screenshot confirming WORM protection on backup copies
Backup monitoring alert configuration in Splunk or backup platform showing on-call escalation for failed backup jobs
Offsite or cross-region backup storage inventory confirming geographically separated copies exist for all BIA-critical systems

title: Redundancy of information processing facilities
description: Information processing facilities must be implemented with sufficient redundancy to meet availability requirements.
guidance: Identify single points of failure for critical services and implement redundancy (active-passive or active-active) per risk appetite.
implementationGuide: Start by mapping all single points of failure (SPOF) for critical information systems through an architecture review, documenting redundancy requirements for compute, storage, networking, and application tiers in a resilience design register. Implement active-active or active-passive redundancy for critical services: deploy application workloads across multiple availability zones in AWS, Azure, or GCP, configure database clustering (AWS RDS Multi-AZ, Azure SQL Geo-Redundant, or PostgreSQL Patroni), and use load balancers (AWS ALB, Azure Application Gateway, or F5 BIG-IP) to distribute traffic and fail over automatically. Configure health checks and automated failover in your load balancer and DNS (AWS Route 53 health checks, Azure Traffic Manager) so that failed instances are removed from rotation within defined thresholds (e.g., two consecutive health check failures in 30 seconds). Monitor system availability using Datadog, Dynatrace, or AWS CloudWatch, defining SLA targets (e.g., 99.9% uptime) and alerting on degraded health before outages become customer-visible. Test failover procedures at least semi-annually through planned failover exercises, documenting the test scope, actual failover time, and any gaps identified against RTO targets. The observable done state is a resilience architecture diagram showing redundancy at every critical tier, completed semi-annual failover test reports, and a monitoring dashboard showing SLA targets met over the preceding 12 months. A common audit failure mode is redundant architecture designed on paper but never failover-tested, so that when a real outage occurs the failover either fails or takes far longer than the documented RTO.
exampleEvidence: AWS or Azure multi-availability-zone deployment configuration showing critical workloads distributed across at least two AZs
Load balancer and auto-scaling configuration screenshot (ALB, Azure Application Gateway) with health check thresholds defined
Datadog, Dynatrace, or CloudWatch availability dashboard showing 12-month uptime metrics against SLA targets
Semi-annual failover test report documenting test scope, actual time to failover, observed RTO vs. target, and sign-off
Database clustering configuration (RDS Multi-AZ or Azure SQL Geo-Redundant) with automated failover policy enabled
Resilience architecture diagram reviewed and approved by the Security Architecture team, identifying redundancy at each system tier

title: Logging
description: Logs that record activities, exceptions, faults, and other relevant events must be produced, stored, protected, and analysed.
guidance: Centralise all security-relevant logs in a SIEM with tamper-proof storage; define and alert on key log-based use cases.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a logging standard that specifies which events must be captured (authentication, authorisation changes, privileged operations, data access, configuration changes, errors), the minimum log fields required (timestamp, user ID, source IP, action, result), the retention period (at least 12 months online, typically 90 days hot), and the log integrity protection mechanism. Deploy a centralised SIEM — Splunk Enterprise Security, Microsoft Sentinel, or IBM QRadar — and onboard all critical log sources: Windows Event Logs, Linux auditd, firewall logs, cloud provider logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log), application logs, and database audit logs. Protect log integrity by streaming logs to an immutable log store (AWS CloudWatch Logs with log group protection, Splunk's index integrity checking, or Azure Monitor Logs with immutability policies) to prevent tampering or deletion. Configure detection rules in the SIEM for critical security events: brute force attempts, privilege escalation, impossible travel, bulk data access, and malware detections, with automated alert routing to the SOC queue. Conduct a quarterly log coverage review to confirm all in-scope systems are forwarding logs within the defined latency SLA (e.g., within 5 minutes of event occurrence) and that no log source has gone silent. The observable done state is a SIEM source inventory showing 100% of in-scope systems onboarded, an immutability configuration audit, and a completed quarterly coverage review with no silent sources. A common audit failure mode is critical systems (legacy servers, network devices) discovered not forwarding logs to the SIEM because they were overlooked during initial onboarding, leaving blind spots that auditors expose by reviewing the SIEM source list against the CMDB.
exampleEvidence: Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, or IBM QRadar log source inventory report listing all onboarded systems and last-event timestamp
Logging standard document defining mandatory event types, required fields, retention periods, and integrity protection requirements
AWS CloudTrail or Azure Activity Log configuration confirming all API actions and management-plane events are captured and retained
SIEM detection rule library screenshot showing alerting rules for brute force, privilege escalation, and bulk data access
Quarterly log coverage review report confirming all in-scope systems forwarding logs within defined latency SLA
Immutable log storage configuration (CloudWatch log group policy or Azure Monitor immutability) preventing log modification or deletion

title: Monitoring activities
description: Networks, systems, and applications must be monitored for anomalous behaviour and appropriate actions taken to evaluate potential information security incidents.
guidance: Define monitoring use cases in the SIEM, tune alert thresholds, and confirm a mean-time-to-detect SLA.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a monitoring policy that specifies which activity types require continuous monitoring (privileged access, authentication events, network traffic anomalies, data exfiltration indicators) and the response SLAs for each alert severity level (critical: 15 minutes, high: 1 hour, medium: 4 hours). Deploy network detection and response (NDR) tools such as Darktrace, Vectra AI, or ExtraHop alongside your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) to identify anomalous network behaviour using machine learning, complementing signature-based SIEM rules with behavioural analytics. Configure user and entity behaviour analytics (UEBA) — either natively in Microsoft Sentinel or via Splunk UBA — to baseline normal user behaviour and alert on deviations such as access at unusual hours, mass file downloads, or lateral movement between systems. Integrate threat intelligence feeds (MISP, Recorded Future, or Microsoft Defender Threat Intelligence) with your SIEM to enrich alerts with known malicious IP, domain, and file hash indicators, enabling automatic correlation and priority escalation. Establish a formal incident response process with runbooks for the top 10 most likely alert types, tested quarterly through tabletop exercises with the SOC and IT teams. The observable done state is a monitoring dashboard showing real-time alert queue status with no alerts exceeding SLA response times, a completed quarterly tabletop exercise report, and an integrated threat intelligence feed actively enriching SIEM detections. A common audit failure mode is a SIEM generating thousands of low-fidelity alerts that the SOC cannot process within SLA, resulting in critical alerts buried in noise — auditors find this by reviewing mean-time-to-acknowledge metrics against the defined SLA.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Sentinel or Splunk SIEM dashboard screenshot showing real-time alert queue with severity classification and SLA countdown
Darktrace, Vectra AI, or ExtraHop NDR configuration and alert sample demonstrating anomalous network behaviour detection
UEBA configuration in Sentinel or Splunk UBA showing behavioural baseline rules and sample high-fidelity alert generated
Threat intelligence feed integration configuration (MISP or Recorded Future) with sample enriched SIEM alert showing IoC correlation
Incident response runbook for a top-priority alert type (e.g., ransomware indicator) reviewed and approved within the last 12 months
Quarterly tabletop exercise report including scenario, participants, actions tested, and identified gaps with remediation plan

title: Clock synchronisation
description: The clocks of information processing systems used by the organisation must be synchronised to approved time sources.
guidance: Configure all servers and network devices to use an internal NTP server synced to a stratum-1 source.
implementationGuide: Start by implementing a Network Time Protocol (NTP) hierarchy with at least two authoritative time sources (GPS-based stratum 1, or cloud provider time services such as AWS Time Sync Service or Azure Time Sync) feeding stratum 2 NTP servers that all infrastructure, cloud instances, and network devices synchronise against. Configure all servers, network devices, and cloud instances to use the defined internal NTP servers exclusively, blocking direct internet NTP access via firewall rules, and validate synchronisation status using monitoring checks in Nagios, Datadog, or SolarWinds that alert when any device's clock drifts more than 500 milliseconds from the reference. Enforce NTP authentication (NTPsec or symmetric key authentication) between internal NTP servers and clients to prevent NTP spoofing attacks that could be used to manipulate audit log timestamps. Document the NTP infrastructure configuration (server topology, authentication configuration, monitoring thresholds) in a runbook stored in the CMDB and reviewed annually. For cloud-native workloads on AWS, Azure, or GCP, confirm that the cloud provider's native time synchronisation is enabled and supplemented by your internal NTP policy. The observable done state is a monitoring report showing 100% of in-scope assets synchronised within the allowed drift threshold, NTP authentication enabled on all internal time servers, and firewall rules blocking direct internet NTP access. A common audit failure mode is endpoints or servers that were migrated from on-premises to cloud but reconfigured to use public NTP servers, resulting in clock inconsistencies that corrupt SIEM event correlation during incident investigation.
exampleEvidence: NTP infrastructure diagram showing stratum 1/2 hierarchy, internal NTP servers, and synchronisation topology
Datadog, SolarWinds, or Nagios NTP monitoring report showing all assets synchronised within drift threshold (≤500ms) for the audit period
Firewall rule export confirming outbound NTP (UDP 123) to internet is blocked, with internal NTP server addresses whitelisted
NTPsec or NTP symmetric key authentication configuration file extract from internal time server
AWS Time Sync Service or Azure Time Sync configuration confirmation for cloud-hosted workloads
Quarterly NTP drift audit report listing any assets exceeding drift threshold and remediation steps taken

title: Use of privileged utility programs
description: The use of utility programs that might be capable of overriding system and application controls must be restricted and tightly controlled.
guidance: Whitelist approved admin utilities and block execution of unapproved system tools via application control policy.
implementationGuide: Start by compiling a complete inventory of all privileged utility programs — password recovery tools, disk editors, network scanners (Nmap, Wireshark), vulnerability scanners, and OS-level administration utilities — and apply the principle of least privilege by restricting installation and execution to authorised security and IT roles only. Implement application allowlisting using Microsoft Defender Application Control (MDAC/WDAC), Carbon Black App Control, or CrowdStrike's application control module to block execution of unapproved utilities on managed endpoints and servers, with policy violations logged to the SIEM. Enforce software restriction policies via Group Policy or Intune that prevent standard users from installing or running utilities outside the approved list, and require a change request and manager approval for any exception. Audit the use of privileged utilities quarterly by reviewing execution logs (Windows Event ID 4688 or Linux auditd exec syscall logs) to detect unauthorised tool usage, correlating with SIEM alerts for tools known to be used in adversary TTPs (Mimikatz, PsExec, etc.). Store approved privileged utilities in a controlled software repository (Artifactory or a secured file share with access logging) and validate checksums before deployment to detect tampering. The observable done state is an allowlist policy covering all managed endpoints, a quarterly usage audit report, and a controlled software repository with access logs showing no unauthorised downloads. A common audit failure mode is finding Nmap, Sysinternals tools, or Wireshark installed on standard user workstations because software installation controls were never enforced, which auditors discover by querying the endpoint management platform for installed software.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft WDAC or Carbon Black App Control policy configuration showing approved utility allowlist and block action for unapproved tools
Privileged utility inventory register listing approved tools, authorised users, business justification, and review date
Intune or Group Policy configuration restricting software installation to administrators only, with policy assignment scope
Quarterly privileged utility usage audit report from SIEM correlating Windows Event 4688 or auditd exec logs against approved list
Artifactory or controlled software repository access log showing utility downloads with user identity and checksum validation
Change request and approval record for a recent privileged utility deployment, demonstrating the formal approval workflow

title: Installation of software on operational systems
description: Procedures and measures must be implemented to securely manage software installation on operational systems.
guidance: Restrict software installation on production systems to administrators and enforce via MDM/endpoint policy.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a software installation policy specifying that only IT-approved software from authorised sources (internal software catalogue, vetted vendor installers) may be installed on corporate systems, that all installations require an approved change request for production systems, and that software must be validated against known-good checksums or signatures before deployment. Enforce software restriction using Microsoft Intune software deployment policies, Jamf software management, or SCCM to provision approved software from a central catalogue, and remove or quarantine any unauthorised software detected by the endpoint management platform. Scan all software repositories and endpoints monthly using Tenable.io or Qualys to detect vulnerable software versions and trigger remediation tickets in ServiceNow or Jira aligned to your vulnerability remediation SLA. Implement application allowlisting on servers using WDAC or SELinux policies to restrict which binaries can execute, reducing the risk of malware or shadow IT installation on critical systems. Maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM) for all critical applications using Syft or CycloneDX, enabling rapid identification of affected systems when a new vulnerability (e.g., Log4Shell) is disclosed. The observable done state is an Intune or Jamf software inventory report showing only approved applications installed on all managed devices, a current SBOM for all critical applications, and zero overdue software vulnerability remediation tickets. A common audit failure mode is personal software (browser extensions, unapproved remote access tools, cryptocurrency miners) discovered on corporate endpoints because software installation controls were set to 'monitor only' and never enforced.
exampleEvidence: Intune or Jamf software inventory report listing installed applications on all managed endpoints, cross-referenced against approved software list
Software installation policy document defining approval requirements, authorised sources, and enforcement mechanism, signed by CISO
SCCM or Intune approved software catalogue screenshot showing available applications for self-service installation
Tenable.io or Qualys software vulnerability scan report for the audit period showing remediation status against SLA
SBOM (CycloneDX or Syft format) for a critical application, demonstrating component-level visibility for vulnerability management
Change request approval record for a recent software installation on a production system, showing change board sign-off

title: Networks security
description: Networks and network devices must be secured, managed, and controlled to protect information in systems and applications.
guidance: Segment networks by sensitivity using VLANs or microsegmentation and enforce deny-by-default firewall rules.
implementationGuide: Start by designing and documenting a network segmentation architecture that separates trust zones — internet-facing DMZ, corporate LAN, production environment, OT/IoT networks, and management plane — using firewalls, VLANs, and zero-trust network access (ZTNA) principles, with all inter-zone traffic explicitly permitted by rule and default-deny applied at every boundary. Deploy next-generation firewalls (Palo Alto Networks NGFW, Fortinet FortiGate, or Check Point NGFW) at each network boundary, with application-layer inspection, threat prevention profiles (IPS, anti-malware, URL filtering) enabled on all inter-zone firewall policies. Implement micro-segmentation for east-west traffic within the production environment using VMware NSX-T, Illumio, or Cisco Tetration to prevent lateral movement by attackers who have breached the perimeter. Conduct quarterly firewall rule reviews to identify and remove overly permissive rules (any-any, unused rules, rules without business justification) using a firewall policy analysis tool such as AlgoSec or Tufin. Monitor all network traffic flowing through firewall boundaries using your SIEM (Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel) with firewall log ingestion and detection rules for port scanning, unusual protocols, and blocked traffic spikes. The observable done state is an approved network segmentation architecture diagram, quarterly firewall rule review completion reports with evidence of rule cleanup, and firewall log ingestion confirmed active in the SIEM. A common audit failure mode is firewall rules created during emergency changes years ago that were never reviewed or removed, resulting in overly permissive rules that violate the documented segmentation policy — auditors find these by requesting a firewall rule export and comparing it to documented business justifications.
exampleEvidence: Network segmentation architecture diagram showing trust zones, firewall boundaries, and VLAN assignments, approved by Security Architecture
Palo Alto or Fortinet NGFW security profile configuration showing IPS, anti-malware, and URL filtering enabled on inter-zone policies
AlgoSec or Tufin quarterly firewall rule review report showing rules analysed, unused rules removed, and any-any rules eliminated
VMware NSX-T or Illumio micro-segmentation policy configuration for production environment east-west traffic control
Splunk or Sentinel firewall log ingestion confirmation with detection rule for port scanning and unusual protocol traffic
Firewall change management log from ServiceNow showing all rule changes within the audit period linked to approved change requests

title: Security of network services
description: Security mechanisms, service levels, and service requirements for all network services must be identified, implemented, and monitored.
guidance: Document agreed security SLAs for all managed network services and include them in supplier contracts.
implementationGuide: Start by inventorying all network services — DNS, DHCP, NTP, web proxies, API gateways, email gateways — and applying the principle of least functionality by disabling or blocking all services not explicitly required for business operations, documented in a network services register with business justification. Secure DNS with DNSSEC to prevent cache poisoning attacks, configure DNS over HTTPS (DoH) or DNS over TLS (DoT) for client resolution, and use a protective DNS service (Cisco Umbrella or Infoblox Threat Defense) to block resolution of known malicious domains as a first-line defence. Enforce all web traffic through a secure web gateway (Zscaler Internet Access, Netskope, or Cisco Umbrella) with SSL/TLS inspection enabled to detect threats hidden in encrypted traffic, URL categorisation policies blocking high-risk categories, and sandboxing for unknown file types. Protect email delivery with SPF, DKIM, and DMARC policies configured to reject (p=reject) for all owned domains, and deploy an email security gateway (Proofpoint, Mimecast, or Microsoft Defender for Office 365) with anti-phishing, anti-spoofing, and attachment sandboxing. Monitor network service availability and anomalies using Datadog Network Performance Monitoring or SolarWinds to detect service disruptions, abuse, or unexpected service additions within minutes. The observable done state is a network services inventory with all services justified and documented, DMARC reporting showing zero SPF/DKIM failures for owned domains, and web proxy logs confirming all user traffic is routed through the secure web gateway. A common audit failure mode is unauthorised or shadow IT services — personal VPN clients, self-hosted DNS resolvers, or rogue Wi-Fi access points — discovered on the network because network service monitoring was only applied to the corporate perimeter, not to internal east-west traffic.
exampleEvidence: Network services register listing all authorised services with business justification, owning team, and last review date
Cisco Umbrella or Infoblox Threat Defense DNS configuration showing protective DNS filtering and DNSSEC validation enabled
Zscaler or Netskope secure web gateway policy configuration showing SSL inspection, URL categorisation, and sandboxing enabled
DMARC record and Proofpoint or Mimecast DMARC report showing p=reject policy enforced for all owned domains with zero failures
SPF and DKIM configuration for all sending domains with DMARC aggregate report confirming full alignment
Datadog or SolarWinds network service monitoring alert configuration with sample alert for unexpected service detection

title: Segregation of networks
description: Groups of information services, users, and information systems must be segregated on networks.
guidance: Place development, test, and production environments in separate network segments with traffic inspection between them.
implementationGuide: Start by defining network segregation requirements based on your information classification policy and risk assessment, documenting which systems must be isolated from others (e.g., PCI cardholder data environment, OT/SCADA networks, guest Wi-Fi, developer networks) and the technical controls required at each boundary. Implement VLAN-based segregation for layer-2 isolation of network segments, with inter-VLAN routing controlled exclusively by NGFW policies (Palo Alto, Fortinet, or Check Point) applying default-deny and explicit permit rules with logging. Deploy a zero-trust network access (ZTNA) solution — Zscaler Private Access, Cloudflare Access, or Palo Alto Prisma Access — to replace broad VPN access with application-specific, identity-aware connections that never expose internal network segments to remote users. For cloud environments, use Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) architecture with separate VPCs or subnets for production, development, and DMZ, enforcing AWS Security Groups and Network ACLs or Azure NSGs to restrict cross-segment traffic. Validate network segregation quarterly through penetration testing or automated network segmentation verification tools (Tufin SecureTrack or Skybox Security) to confirm that isolation controls are functioning as designed and that no unauthorised paths exist between segments. The observable done state is a network segregation architecture diagram validated by quarterly verification testing, NGFW policy reports showing default-deny with explicit permits, and ZTNA deployment confirmed as the sole remote access method. A common audit failure mode is a development or test network that shares the same VLAN as production because a temporary connection was created during a project and never removed — auditors find this by reviewing VLAN assignments and comparing against the segmentation diagram.
exampleEvidence: Network segregation architecture diagram showing VLAN assignments, trust boundaries, and inter-segment firewall policies
NGFW policy export (Palo Alto or Fortinet) showing default-deny between segments with explicit business-justified permit rules
Zscaler Private Access or Cloudflare Access ZTNA configuration replacing VPN for remote access to internal applications
AWS VPC or Azure VNet architecture screenshot showing separate environments (prod, dev, DMZ) with NSG/Security Group isolation
Tufin SecureTrack or Skybox quarterly network segmentation verification report confirming no unauthorised inter-segment paths
OT/SCADA network isolation configuration showing air-gap or data-diode boundary between operational and corporate networks

title: Web filtering
description: Access to external websites must be managed to reduce exposure to malicious content.
guidance: Deploy a web proxy or DNS filtering solution to block malicious, C2, and high-risk categories by default.
implementationGuide: Start by deploying a web filtering solution — Zscaler Internet Access, Cisco Umbrella, or Netskope — that enforces URL categorisation policies for all users and devices, blocking high-risk categories (malware sites, phishing, anonymous proxies, illegal content) by default and requiring business justification for any category exemption. Configure SSL/TLS inspection on the web filter to examine encrypted HTTPS traffic (which constitutes the majority of web traffic) for embedded threats, with inspection exemption lists limited to banking, healthcare, and other privacy-sensitive categories defined in an approved exemption policy. Implement DNS-layer filtering as a complementary control using Cisco Umbrella or Infoblox Threat Defense to block malicious domains at resolution, providing protection before any connection is established, and integrate with threat intelligence feeds for real-time IOC blocking. Apply web filtering policies to remote and mobile users by routing their traffic through the ZTNA or cloud proxy (Zscaler, Netskope) regardless of location, eliminating the 'off-network' bypass that traditional on-premises proxies create. Review web filtering logs in Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel monthly to identify users with high block rates (possible targeted attack or policy abuse), newly blocked categories requested for business access, and any configuration bypasses. The observable done state is a web filtering coverage report showing 100% of managed users subject to policy regardless of location, a current category policy document with business-justified exceptions, and monthly log review records. A common audit failure mode is remote workers' devices bypassing the web filter entirely because split-tunnel VPN configuration routes only corporate traffic through the proxy, leaving internet-bound traffic uncontrolled.
exampleEvidence: Zscaler Internet Access or Cisco Umbrella URL categorisation policy screenshot showing blocked categories and approved exemptions
SSL/TLS inspection policy configuration showing HTTPS traffic inspection enabled with defined exemption categories
DNS filtering configuration (Umbrella or Infoblox) showing malicious domain blocking with threat intelligence feed integration
Web filtering coverage report showing all managed users (including remote workers) subject to policy via cloud proxy or ZTNA
Monthly web filtering log review summary from Splunk or Sentinel identifying block events, user risk scores, and policy exceptions
Category exception request and approval record demonstrating business-justified bypass through a formal approval process

title: Use of cryptography
description: Rules for the effective use of cryptography, including cryptographic key management, must be defined and implemented.
guidance: Publish a cryptography policy specifying approved algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+, TLS 1.2+) and key lifecycle management procedures.
implementationGuide: Start by publishing an approved cryptography standard that defines algorithm selections for each use case (data at rest: AES-256-GCM; data in transit: TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred; key exchange: ECDHE; signatures: RSA-4096 or ECDSA P-384), prohibits weak algorithms (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0/1.1, RC4, MD5, SHA-1 for signatures), and mandates key lengths meeting current NIST SP 800-57 guidance. Centralise key management in an HSM-backed service — AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault HSM, or HashiCorp Vault with HSM seal — ensuring all application encryption keys are generated, stored, and rotated within the KMS, never extracted into application code or configuration. Enforce TLS configuration across all internet-facing and internal services using a hardened TLS profile tested with sslyze, Qualys SSL Labs (targeting A+ rating), or testssl.sh, and automate certificate deployment and renewal through Venafi or Let's Encrypt with ACME. Integrate code-level cryptography checks into your CI/CD pipeline using Semgrep or SonarQube rules that flag use of prohibited algorithms or hardcoded key material before code reaches production. Conduct an annual cryptography review with security architecture input on post-quantum readiness (NIST PQC finalist algorithms: CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium) and update the approved algorithm register accordingly. The observable done state is a Qualys SSL Labs A+ rating for all internet-facing services, a KMS key inventory with defined rotation schedules, and a completed annual cryptography review document. A common audit failure mode is legacy internal services discovered still using TLS 1.0 or self-signed certificates because they were excluded from the TLS hardening project scope — auditors find these by running an internal sslyze scan across all RFC 1918 address ranges.
exampleEvidence: Cryptography standard document listing approved algorithms by use case, prohibited algorithms, and annual review history
Qualys SSL Labs or sslyze scan results for all internet-facing services showing A or A+ ratings with TLS 1.3 preferred
AWS KMS or Azure Key Vault key inventory showing all keys with algorithm, key length, creation date, and rotation policy
Venafi or Let's Encrypt ACME certificate lifecycle report showing automated renewal with zero expired certificates
Semgrep or SonarQube cryptography scan results from CI/CD pipeline showing zero prohibited algorithm findings in code
Annual cryptography review document including post-quantum readiness assessment and updated approved algorithm register

title: Secure development lifecycle
description: Rules for the secure development of software and systems must be established and applied.
guidance: Integrate SAST, SCA, and DAST tools into the CI/CD pipeline and enforce security gates before production deployment.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing a secure software development lifecycle (SDLC) policy that mandates security activities at each phase: threat modelling at design, SAST scanning during development, SCA scanning for dependencies, DAST scanning before release, and security sign-off before production deployment. Integrate SAST tooling — Semgrep, Checkmarx, or SonarQube — into your CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or Azure DevOps) with pipeline policies that fail the build on any new critical or high-severity finding, preventing vulnerable code from reaching the main branch. Conduct threat modelling for all significant new features and system changes using the STRIDE methodology or OWASP Threat Dragon, documenting identified threats, risk ratings, and mitigating controls before development begins, with output reviewed by the Security Architecture team. Train all developers in secure coding practices annually using interactive platforms (Secure Code Warrior, Snyk Learn, or Checkmarx Codebuster), and track completion in the LMS with a minimum passing score requirement. Establish a security champions programme with at least one trained security champion per development team who acts as the first line of defence for security issues and facilitates threat modelling and code review. The observable done state is a SAST scan integrated into every repository's CI/CD pipeline with zero critical findings in production, a completed developer security training report, and threat model documents for all major features released in the audit period. A common audit failure mode is SAST scanning configured in pipelines but set to non-blocking mode, meaning developers can ignore findings and deploy vulnerable code without any friction — auditors find this by reviewing pipeline configuration files and requesting a report of findings that were bypassed.
exampleEvidence: Secure SDLC policy document defining mandatory security activities at each development phase, approved by CISO
Semgrep or Checkmarx CI/CD pipeline configuration showing blocking policy on critical/high SAST findings with sample failed build
Threat model document for a major feature (STRIDE analysis, identified mitigations) reviewed by Security Architecture
LMS developer secure coding training completion report showing 100% of developers completed training with passing scores
Security champions programme charter listing champions per team, training completed, and activities performed in the audit period
SAST scan trend report showing reduction in new critical findings over the audit period, demonstrating programme effectiveness

title: Application security requirements
description: Information security requirements must be identified, specified, and approved when developing or acquiring applications.
guidance: Include security requirements (OWASP Top 10 mitigations) in functional specifications and validate them during testing.
implementationGuide: Start by defining application security requirements derived from business risk, regulatory requirements (GDPR, PCI DSS), and the OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS), and include these requirements as acceptance criteria in every user story or feature specification before development begins. Implement DAST scanning using OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite Enterprise, or HCL AppScan against staging environments as part of the release pipeline, with findings triaged and critical or high severity issues required to be resolved before production deployment approval. Conduct annual application penetration tests for all internet-facing and high-risk internal applications, using a qualified external provider (CREST-certified), with findings tracked to closure in the vulnerability management system. Integrate software composition analysis (SCA) using Snyk Open Source, Dependabot, or OWASP Dependency-Check into the build pipeline to identify and alert on known-vulnerable third-party libraries, with automated pull requests to update affected dependencies. Define a bug bounty or responsible disclosure policy for externally accessible applications, and establish a process to triage, validate, and remediate externally reported vulnerabilities within defined SLAs. The observable done state is application security requirements documented for all applications, a completed annual pen test with remediation evidence, and SCA scanning active in all production application pipelines with zero open critical dependency vulnerabilities. A common audit failure mode is third-party library vulnerabilities (like Log4Shell or Spring4Shell) persisting in production for months beyond disclosure because the SCA scanning was only run at build time and the built artefacts were never re-scanned against updated vulnerability databases.
exampleEvidence: Application security requirements document aligned to OWASP ASVS, incorporated into project acceptance criteria for all applications
OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite Enterprise DAST scan report for a staging environment showing finding status before production release
Annual penetration test report (CREST-certified provider) for a critical application with remediation closure evidence
Snyk or Dependabot SCA scan results in CI/CD pipeline showing dependency vulnerability findings and automated PR remediation
Vulnerability remediation tracking report in Jira or ServiceNow showing all critical application security findings resolved within SLA
Responsible disclosure or bug bounty policy document and vulnerability triage log showing received reports and response timeline

title: Secure system architecture and engineering principles
description: Principles for engineering secure systems must be established, documented, maintained, and applied to any information system implementation effort.
guidance: Adopt and communicate secure-by-design principles (zero trust, least privilege, defence in depth) for all new systems.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing and publishing a secure architecture framework aligned to TOGAF or SABSA that defines security principles (defence in depth, least privilege, zero trust, fail-secure), mandatory security controls for each system tier (web, application, database, infrastructure), and architecture review gates at each SDLC phase. Conduct security architecture reviews for all new systems and significant changes using a standardised review checklist (covering authentication, authorisation, data encryption, audit logging, network segmentation, and API security), with documented approval by the Security Architecture team before design is finalised. Enforce API security standards — OAuth 2.0 / OIDC for authentication, input validation, rate limiting, and TLS enforcement — on all APIs using an API gateway (AWS API Gateway, Apigee, or Kong) with security policies applied as gateway-level enforcement, not relying solely on application-level controls. Implement a security design review process for cloud architecture using AWS Well-Architected Tool or Azure Advisor Security pillar assessments, generating a formal review report with identified risks and remediation recommendations. Maintain an approved technology reference architecture document that is reviewed annually by the Security Architecture Committee and updated to reflect evolving threat landscape and technology changes. The observable done state is a security architecture review sign-off for every system entering production in the audit period, a completed AWS Well-Architected or Azure Advisor assessment, and a published and approved technology reference architecture document. A common audit failure mode is new cloud workloads deployed without passing through the security architecture review gate because the review process was only defined for on-premises systems and was never extended to cover cloud-native deployments.
exampleEvidence: Security architecture framework document defining principles, mandatory control tiers, and review gate requirements, approved by CISO
Security architecture review checklist and completed sign-off for at least one new system deployed during the audit period
AWS Well-Architected Tool or Azure Advisor Security pillar assessment report for a production workload with remediation plan
API gateway (AWS API Gateway or Kong) security policy configuration showing OAuth 2.0, input validation, and rate limiting enforced
Technology reference architecture document showing approved patterns for authentication, encryption, and network segmentation
Security Architecture Committee meeting minutes from annual architecture review with updated decisions and rationale

title: Secure coding
description: Secure coding principles must be applied to software development.
guidance: Train developers on OWASP Secure Coding Practices and enforce peer code review for all changes to security-sensitive code.
implementationGuide: Start by establishing organisational secure coding standards based on OWASP Top 10, SANS CWE Top 25, and language-specific security guides (e.g., Google Java Security Guide, Microsoft Secure Coding Guidelines for C#), and require all developers to acknowledge and follow these standards as part of their role onboarding. Enforce automated code quality and security checks at the IDE level using plugins (SonarLint, Snyk IDE plugin) that provide real-time feedback to developers as they code, before code is even committed, reducing the cost of defect remediation. Implement mandatory peer code review for all code changes via pull requests in GitHub or GitLab, with security-relevant changes (authentication, authorisation, cryptography, input validation) requiring review by a certified security champion or security engineer before merge. Use Semgrep or Checkmarx with custom rule sets to enforce organisation-specific secure coding patterns (e.g., banned function usage, mandatory input sanitisation wrappers, required logging for security events) in the CI/CD pipeline. Track secure coding defect metrics — number of security findings per code change, time-to-fix, defect density by team — monthly and report them to development leadership to drive continuous improvement. The observable done state is SAST scanning blocking merges on critical findings in all repositories, 100% of code changes reviewed before merge, and a monthly secure coding metrics dashboard reviewed by development leadership. A common audit failure mode is secure coding standards existing as a PDF document that no developer has read, with no automated enforcement — auditors find this by reviewing pipeline configurations for SAST integration and asking developers what the standards require.
exampleEvidence: Secure coding standards document based on OWASP Top 10 and CWE Top 25, with developer acknowledgement log
GitHub or GitLab branch protection configuration requiring at least one approved code review before merge to main branch
Semgrep or Checkmarx custom rule set configuration for organisation-specific secure coding patterns with sample blocked PR
SonarLint or Snyk IDE plugin deployment report showing installation on all developer workstations via Intune or Jamf
Monthly secure coding metrics dashboard showing SAST finding count, time-to-fix, and defect density trend by team
Security champion code review log showing security-relevant pull requests reviewed by a certified champion before merge

title: Security testing in development and acceptance
description: Security testing processes must be defined and implemented in the development lifecycle.
guidance: Define minimum security test criteria for user acceptance testing; block go-live if critical findings remain unresolved.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a security testing policy that mandates the types of testing required at each SDLC stage (SAST in development, DAST in staging, penetration testing pre-release for high-risk systems, red team exercises annually) and the acceptance criteria for moving between stages (no open critical or high findings without approved risk acceptance). Integrate automated security testing into every CI/CD pipeline: SAST with Semgrep or Checkmarx at the PR stage, SCA with Snyk or Dependabot at the build stage, container image scanning with Trivy or Prisma Cloud at the packaging stage, and infrastructure-as-code scanning with Checkov or tfsec before deployment. Commission annual external penetration tests from a CREST or GPEN-certified provider for all internet-facing applications and critical internal systems, with scope and methodology agreed in advance, findings tracked in the vulnerability management system, and remediation evidence provided to the provider for report closure. Conduct internal red team or purple team exercises at least annually to test detection and response capabilities, simulating realistic adversary TTPs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, with findings shared with the SOC to improve detection rule coverage. Maintain a security test results register in Jira or ServiceNow tracking all findings, severity, owner, target remediation date, and actual closure date, with overdue items escalated to the CISO monthly. The observable done state is automated security testing active in every production pipeline with zero bypass mechanisms, a completed annual penetration test with remediation evidence, and a security test results register with no overdue critical findings. A common audit failure mode is 'scope creep exclusions' — systems that were excluded from the penetration test scope for operational reasons but never tested by any other means, creating an unexamined attack surface auditors flag immediately.
exampleEvidence: Security testing policy defining required test types per SDLC stage, acceptance criteria, and penetration test frequency
CI/CD pipeline security testing configuration showing SAST, SCA, container scan, and IaC scan stages with blocking policy
Annual external penetration test report (CREST-certified) with scope, methodology, findings, and remediation closure evidence
Trivy or Prisma Cloud container image scan results in pipeline showing zero critical CVEs in production images
Red team or purple team exercise report with MITRE ATT&CK TTP mapping, detection gaps identified, and SOC improvement actions
Security test results register showing all findings for the audit period with severity, owner, target date, and closure status

title: Outsourced development
description: The organisation must direct, monitor, and review the activities related to outsourced system development.
guidance: Include security requirements, code review rights, and SAST obligations in contracts with outsourced development vendors.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a third-party access policy that covers all outsourced IT functions and external suppliers with access to your systems, requiring security assessments before onboarding, contractual security obligations, and periodic reassessment aligned to the risk level of the relationship. Conduct due diligence for all new outsourced development or managed service providers by reviewing their ISO 27001 certification, SOC 2 Type II report, or equivalent, and performing a vendor risk assessment questionnaire (aligned to CAIQ or SIG) before contract signature. Include mandatory security clauses in all outsourcing contracts: right-to-audit, security incident notification within 72 hours, data handling obligations, access control requirements, and subcontractor restriction or approval clauses, reviewed by Legal and the CISO. Monitor outsourced supplier activity using privileged access management (CyberArk) with session recording for all third-party privileged sessions, and review access entitlements for all outsourced personnel quarterly to confirm only currently active contractors retain access. Conduct annual security performance reviews for all high-risk outsourced relationships, reviewing SLA performance, incident history, security assessment findings, and any contractual obligation breaches. The observable done state is a third-party register showing security assessment status, contract clause checklist completion, and quarterly access review for every outsourced supplier with system access. A common audit failure mode is departed contractor accounts remaining active weeks after contract end because there is no automated offboarding trigger from the procurement system to the identity management platform — auditors find these by comparing active accounts against current contract status.
exampleEvidence: Third-party risk register listing all outsourced suppliers with risk tier, security assessment date, and next review date
Vendor security assessment report (CAIQ or SIG questionnaire) and ISO 27001 or SOC 2 Type II certificate for a high-risk supplier
Outsourcing contract extract showing mandatory security clauses: right-to-audit, incident notification, data handling, and access controls
CyberArk session recording policy configuration for all third-party privileged access with sample session metadata log
Quarterly third-party access recertification report confirming active contractor accounts match current contract roster
Annual supplier security performance review report with SLA compliance, incident history, and remediation tracking

title: Separation of development, test and production environments
description: Development, testing, and production environments must be separated and secured.
guidance: Enforce environment separation via separate accounts/subscriptions and prohibit production data in dev/test without masking.
implementationGuide: Start by mapping all production, development, test, and staging environments in your CMDB and enforcing a separation policy that prohibits using production data in non-production environments without formal anonymisation, prevents developers from having direct production system access, and requires separate credentials and network paths for each environment. Implement environment separation in cloud platforms using separate AWS accounts or Azure subscriptions per environment (production, staging, development), enforced via AWS Organizations service control policies (SCPs) or Azure Management Group policies that restrict cross-environment resource access. Configure separate identity provider groups and access policies for each environment in Microsoft Entra ID or Okta, with production access requiring MFA, just-in-time elevation via PIM, and manager approval, while development environments use standard RBAC with no elevated privileges. Automate environment provisioning and promotion using CI/CD pipelines (GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, or Azure DevOps) with promotion gates that require successful security scans and QA sign-off before code advances from development to staging to production. Audit environment separation controls quarterly by reviewing cloud account access logs, CI/CD pipeline promotion logs, and identity provider access reports to confirm no cross-environment credential sharing or unauthorised production access. The observable done state is separate cloud accounts or subscriptions per environment confirmed in AWS Organizations or Azure Management Groups, CI/CD pipeline promotion gates enforced, and a quarterly audit report showing no cross-environment access violations. A common audit failure mode is developers having direct SSH or console access to production servers 'just in case' — auditors find these standing access accounts by reviewing bastion host access logs or cloud IAM reports for developer role assignments in production accounts.
exampleEvidence: AWS Organizations or Azure Management Group structure showing separate accounts/subscriptions per environment with SCP/policy restrictions
Microsoft Entra ID or Okta access group configuration showing separate groups for production vs. non-production with different MFA and JIT requirements
CI/CD pipeline promotion gate configuration showing required security scan and QA sign-off before production deployment
CMDB environment register listing all environments with associated systems, access controls, and data classification
Quarterly environment separation audit report showing no developer accounts with standing production access
Production data anonymisation procedure confirming data masking is applied before any data is copied to non-production environments

title: Change management
description: Changes to information processing facilities and information systems must be subject to change management procedures.
guidance: Enforce a change advisory board (CAB) approval process for all changes to production systems with mandatory rollback plans.
implementationGuide: Start by implementing a formal change management process in ServiceNow or Jira Service Management that categorises changes by risk (standard, normal, emergency), requires risk assessment and rollback plan documentation for all normal and emergency changes, and mandates independent approval from a Change Advisory Board (CAB) or designated approver before implementation. Enforce change control at the technical level by granting production system access only through the change management workflow: changes to infrastructure are applied exclusively via approved IaC pipelines (Terraform, Ansible) triggered by an approved change ticket, and direct console or SSH access to production requires a time-limited just-in-time access request tied to the change ticket. Monitor all production changes in real time using AWS Config, Azure Policy, or your CMDB integration to detect and alert on any configuration change that was not preceded by an approved change record — these 'unauthorised changes' are automatically escalated to the change manager and CISO. Conduct a post-implementation review (PIR) for all major changes and all emergency changes within five business days of completion, documenting whether the change achieved its objective, whether rollback was required, and any lessons learned. Maintain a change freeze calendar covering critical business periods (financial year-end, major product launches) and enforce freeze windows by disabling production deployment pipelines via pipeline environment protection rules in GitHub or GitLab. The observable done state is a change management report showing 100% of production changes preceded by an approved change ticket, a PIR completion rate of 100% for major and emergency changes, and zero unauthorised changes detected by configuration monitoring in the audit period. A common audit failure mode is emergency change procedures used for routine changes to bypass CAB review — auditors find this by reviewing the ratio of emergency to normal changes and asking for post-implementation reviews that justify the emergency classification.
exampleEvidence: ServiceNow or Jira change management workflow configuration showing risk tiers, approval requirements, and CAB process
AWS Config or Azure Policy change detection report for the audit period confirming zero changes without a linked approved change ticket
Terraform or Ansible CI/CD pipeline requiring approved change ticket number before production deployment job executes
Post-implementation review (PIR) completion log for all major and emergency changes in the audit period
Change freeze calendar and pipeline environment protection rules in GitHub/GitLab disabling production deployments during freeze windows
Monthly change management KPI report showing change success rate, emergency change ratio, and unauthorised change count

title: Test information
description: Test information must be appropriately selected, protected, and managed.
guidance: Prohibit use of live personal data in testing; use synthetic or anonymised data sets approved by the data owner.
implementationGuide: Start by defining a test information policy that identifies which categories of test data are permitted (synthetic data, anonymised production data) and which are prohibited (real PII, payment card data, health records), and embed this requirement into your SDLC policy and developer onboarding documentation. Generate synthetic test data using tools such as Mockaroo, Faker libraries (Python/Java), or Broadcom Test Data Manager to create realistic but non-sensitive datasets that cover all test scenarios without exposing personal or sensitive information. Where production-like data is necessary for testing, enforce masking through Delphix, IBM InfoSphere Optim, or database-native masking (Oracle Data Masking, SQL Server Dynamic Data Masking) before provisioning data to any non-production environment, with the masking job run logged and verified. Scan all non-production environments monthly using data discovery tools such as Spirion, BigID, or AWS Macie to detect any unmasked PII or sensitive data that has been introduced without authorisation, treating findings as security incidents requiring immediate remediation. Control access to test environments and test data using the same RBAC principles as production, preventing test data from being exported to personal devices or unapproved storage locations through Purview DLP policies. The observable done state is a test data management policy applied to all development teams, monthly data discovery scans showing zero unmasked PII in test environments, and masking job execution logs for all data provisioned to non-production environments. A common audit failure mode is developers using personal cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox) to transfer production CSV exports to their local test environment for convenience — auditors find this by reviewing DLP logs or asking developers directly during interviews.
exampleEvidence: Test data management policy document defining permitted and prohibited data types for non-production environments
Delphix or IBM InfoSphere Optim masking job execution log showing all data provisioned to test environments was masked before use
Mockaroo or Faker-based synthetic data generation script in version control, demonstrating use of synthetic rather than production data
Spirion, BigID, or AWS Macie monthly scan report for non-production environments confirming zero unmasked PII detected
Microsoft Purview DLP policy blocking export of sensitive data from test environments to personal cloud storage or USB
Access control report for test environments showing RBAC group membership and no sharing of production system credentials

title: Protection of information systems during audit testing
description: Audit tests and other assurance activities involving assessment of operational systems must be planned and agreed between the tester and appropriate management.
guidance: Require written approval from system owners before any penetration test or audit scan is conducted against production.
implementationGuide: Start by defining an audit scope register that lists all systems subject to audit activity monitoring, the types of audit access permitted (read-only, authenticated scans, log access), the tools authorised for audit use, and the controls in place to protect audit tools and data from tampering. Protect audit infrastructure — SIEM platforms (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel), vulnerability scanners (Tenable, Qualys), and log repositories — by hosting them in an isolated management network segment with access restricted to the security operations team via privileged access management (CyberArk), with all access to audit systems logged and reviewed. Configure your SIEM and audit tooling to alert on any attempt to modify, delete, or disable audit logs or audit configurations, treating these events as critical security incidents requiring immediate SOC response and escalation to the CISO. Restrict audit scanner accounts to read-only permissions on target systems, rotating scanner credentials via HashiCorp Vault on a 90-day schedule, and never using scanner accounts for operational tasks. Conduct an annual review of the audit scope register to confirm all new systems have been added, audit tool configurations are current and hardened, and audit data retention meets the defined 12-month minimum. The observable done state is an audit scope register covering all in-scope systems, audit tooling hosted in an isolated segment with PAM-controlled access, and SIEM alerting on audit log tampering with zero unreviewed critical alerts. A common audit failure mode is audit log repositories accessible to the system administrators being audited, creating the ability to tamper with evidence — auditors assess this by reviewing who has delete or modify permissions on the SIEM log index and whether those users are also system administrators of in-scope systems.
exampleEvidence: Audit scope register listing all systems subject to monitoring, authorised tools, access types, and responsible team
Network diagram showing audit infrastructure (SIEM, scanners) hosted in isolated management segment with PAM-controlled access
Splunk or Microsoft Sentinel alert rule for audit log modification or deletion events with sample alert and escalation workflow
CyberArk or HashiCorp Vault configuration showing audit scanner account credentials rotated on 90-day schedule with access logs
SIEM log index access control report confirming system administrators of audited systems have no modify/delete permissions on audit logs
Annual audit scope register review meeting minutes confirming all new systems added, tool configurations reviewed and approved
